# PR Quality Gate - Pattern Catalog

Empirical catalog of patterns the GitHub Copilot PR reviewer flags across consuming projects. Each entry is a single markdown table row + JSON `params` per the schema in `README.md` §"Catalog grammar".

This catalog is **project-deidentified**: signatures and patterns are abstract; consuming projects do not need to customize it. FP entries are inline (no separate file).

<!-- Schema reminder (per README.md §Catalog grammar):
     | slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
     Pipe characters inside JSON values escaped as \|. Parser unescapes before JSON.parse.
     Tier (6th column) added on lightweight-gate-v5. Values: HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW.
     Legacy 5-cell rows default to tier=MEDIUM with a stderr diagnostic; sunset by next catalog edit. -->

## Tier semantics (added with 6-cell schema bump)

Tier controls cost optimization in lighter panel modes AND `HIGH-TIER-SLUGS.md` generation:

- **HIGH** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): rg-rules run in every mode (full/triage/lint-only); review-pass-only emit `unreviewed_review_pass_only_slugs` warning in lint-only.
- **MEDIUM** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): run in full/triage only; SKIP in lint-only.
- **LOW** (rg-rules + review-pass-only): run in full only; SKIP in triage and lint-only.

Tier assignment (manual, heuristic-guided; `scripts/sync-critical-rules.ps1` (or its bash twin `scripts/sync-critical-rules.sh`) consumes it verbatim):
- The `tier` column is set by the agent at catalog time, applying a frequency heuristic as a GUIDELINE - for rg-detectable rules (`scope_mode: diff-scoped|tree-scoped|hybrid`) the rg-corpus hit count; for review-pass-only rules the `panel-misses.csv` unique `pr_ref` count (not raw row count).
- Guideline bands: >=5 = HIGH, 3-4 = MEDIUM, 0-2 = LOW. Impact/breadth is a sanctioned MEDIUM-over-LOW override - tier is a triage-inclusion judgment (which slugs run in lighter panel modes), not a blind count.
- Script role: `scripts/sync-critical-rules.ps1` (and its bash twin `scripts/sync-critical-rules.sh`, which produces byte-identical output) reads the `tier` column VERBATIM and filters the HIGH-tier `review-pass-only` rows into `HIGH-TIER-SLUGS.md` (HIGH rows whose `scope_mode` is not `review-pass-only` - rg-detectable and checker-scoped - are excluded by that scope filter); it reads no `panel-misses.csv` and derives no tier (the heuristic is an authoring guideline, not a script step).
- **Catalog-first inclusion (zero-repeats):** EVERY generalizable review finding is cataloged immediately (1-hit), NOT gated by a frequency threshold; see `.github/playbooks/instruction-set-maintenance.md`. Frequency only INFORMS tier/priority (a guideline, above) and the section grouping below (the seed-corpus frequency labels are historical). New entries are placed by role (rg-detectable -> battery section; judgment -> review-pass-only) and tiered by the manual, heuristic-guided assignment above.

## Patterns (high-frequency battery, ≥5 hits in seed corpus - frequency label historical; inclusion is catalog-first per Tier semantics above)

| slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| doc-impl-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every modified XML doc comment, README/docs/playbook text, or PR description claim: read each concrete prose claim and verify the code actually does what it says. The XML doc forms in scope are summary, remarks, param, returns, see cref, and any other doc tag; doc-impl-mismatch owns ALL XML doc comments (claim falsification, even if a refactor moved the doc), while inline code comments and config-file comments belong to `code-comment-mismatch` and runtime-emitted user-facing/log/error strings belong to `message-asserts-outcome-not-guaranteed-on-every-branch`. Common failure modes: XML doc claims thread-context that captured SC contradicts; PR description names a feature the markup does not render; `<see cref>` references that won't resolve; "this delegates to X via COM" when X uses procedural API. **Interface-extension sub-case** (frequently missed): when an interface or class gets NEW members in the diff (`HasActiveItems` + `ReloadAllActiveItemsAsync` added to an interface whose summary previously described only the older concern), verify the interface-level `<summary>` enumerates / describes the new members or at least acknowledges the new responsibility surface. A summary that still describes only the pre-extension subset is `doc-impl-mismatch`. **DI-registration-method contract sub-case** (frequently missed): when a DI registration method (`AddXyz` extension method on `IServiceCollection`, equivalent on `IHostBuilder`, `IApplicationBuilder.UseXyz`, ASP.NET Core `services.AddOptions<T>()`, Autofac `ContainerBuilder.RegisterXyz`, etc.) has an XML summary that documents required external registrations / host-provided dependencies (typical "Callers MUST also register: AddFluxor, AddXxx, ..." pattern), verify that EVERY new singleton/scoped/transient registration in the diff which depends on a host-provided abstraction is reflected in the contract doc. Adding a new registration that depends on `IHostProvidedService` without updating the "callers must register" list means a downstream host calling the registration method gets a DI activation failure at runtime instead of a compile-time / config-time signal. **Runnable-example sub-case** (frequently missed in instruction docs / playbooks / READMEs as well as source comments): when the diff adds or edits a snippet the reader is MEANT TO RUN AS-IS (a playbook step, a copy-paste command, a `run:` line, a PowerShell / bash one-liner, a regex) AND the surrounding prose CLAIMS a specific effect or output, verify the snippet's ACTUAL EXECUTION SEMANTICS produce that effect - DRY-RUN it in your head (parse the real tokenization / escaping / flags), do NOT just eyeball whether it "looks like" the prose. A plausible-reading-but-non-functional snippet is a doc-impl-mismatch INVISIBLE to a surface read: e.g. PowerShell `-replace CRLF,LF` looks like it normalizes line endings, but the bare word CRLF is not a value expression after `-replace`, so the snippet does not even PARSE (PowerShell: "You must provide a value expression following the -replace operator") and normalizes nothing. **Audit method** (general default - the Runnable-example sub-case's in-head dry-run still applies to executable snippets, which a token-grep cannot falsify): for every prose claim naming a UI element, API symbol, service, command, artifact, or file token, extract the token and grep the diff/codebase; if the token is absent, renamed, or the code contradicts the claim, flag. For a docs-only/README change, do NOT surface-read - run this extract-and-grep per claim before accepting the file. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: illustrative / pseudocode blocks, snippets explicitly marked not-to-run, or a block with no claimed effect to contradict; and do not flag aspirational roadmap prose, explicitly approximate summaries, or user-facing simplifications unless a specific named behavior, condition, message, artifact, or command is contradicted. Flag every prose-vs-code divergence. |  | HIGH |
| comment-necessity | review-pass-only | {} | **CITATION-FIRST:** Every NEW or substantively rewritten comment in the diff MUST cite an `approval_turn:` field in the §2.6 audit ledger (in this instructions repo a LOCAL git note; the gitignored receipt `.github/pr-quality-gate/audits/last.md` is the authoring surface, flushed by the post-commit hook). Valid `approval_turn:` values: (i) a real `ask_user` approval-turn/message ref PAIRED with an `allowed-case` (non-obvious invariant \| external constraint \| trade-off); (ii) `n/a - exempt: <category>` using one of the 6 canonical tokens (`typo` \| `deletion` \| `stale-comment-fix-per-§3.9/§3.10` \| `generated` \| `vendored` \| `THROWAWAY-header`); (iii) `n/a - degraded-mode-drop` (headless predicate matched); (iv) `n/a - no-response-drop` (ask_user attempted but user didn't respond); (v) `deleted (...)` (audit-trail-only for dropped proposals). **Absent audit record, missing `approval_turn:` value, or invalid exempt category = violation.** Comment-syntax scope: `//` inline, `/* */` block, `///` XML doc, `#` line (Python/Bash/PowerShell/YAML/HCL/Terraform/Dockerfile), `<!-- -->` (HTML/XML/Markdown/Razor), `--` (SQL/Lua/Haskell/Ada/Elm), `<# #>` (PowerShell), `;` (Lisp/Clojure/INI), docstrings (Python `"""..."""`, Elixir `@moduledoc`/`@doc`), JSDoc/TSDoc `/** */`, godoc, Rustdoc (`///` outer, `//!` inner) - NOT pre-existing comments in the baseline that happen to live in modified files. Beyond the approval check, ALSO flag comments that (a) restate what the code obviously does, (b) reference panel artifacts (slot/round numbers, PR-bundle labels, panel-phase names, "the bot caught this"), (c) duplicate XML method summaries with inline comments, (d) include multi-line XML `<remarks>` blocks on internal types, (e) narrate refactor/PR history inline, (f) explain a contract that the method/test NAME already conveys, (g) are XML doc on a `private` member (categorical ban per `csharp.instructions.md` - step-3 unreachable), (h) are XML doc on a NEW public/internal API absent the narrow `csharp.instructions.md` default-OFF allowed scenarios (non-obvious failure mode, thread-safety guarantee, BCL quirk, spec reference) with explicit step-3 user approval. **Acceptable** = only the three §3.1 *Allowed* cases that survived step 3: a non-obvious algorithmic invariant the code cannot self-document, an external-constraint workaround (spec/BCL-quirk/version citation), or a deliberate trade-off the reader would otherwise question. XML `<summary>` on public APIs is NOT independently acceptable here; follows `csharp.instructions.md` default-OFF + step-3 user approval. |  | HIGH |
| comment-hygiene-purge-survivor | review-pass-only | {} | Comment-hygiene purge runs as post-code-change step 2.4 BEFORE this panel: a whole-touched-file DELETE-only sweep over EVERY comment (new AND pre-existing) in each touched file (see `comment-hygiene-purge.md`). For THIS rule, open EACH touched file in FULL - comments on UNCHANGED lines are in scope, NOT just the diff hunks (this is the gap `comment-necessity` leaves: it excludes pre-existing comments in modified files). Flag, as the purge having MISSED a site: (a) any surviving comment that merely restates what the code / name already says; (b) any multi-line XML / doc narrative summary on a self-evident member; (c) any kept comment whose semantic content exceeds one terse line WITHOUT a load-bearing why (the only acceptable whys are the three AGENTS.md §3.1 *Allowed* cases - non-obvious algorithmic invariant, external-constraint workaround, deliberate trade-off). A surviving restatement comment is a BLOCKING finding. Comment-syntax + file scope follow `comment-protocol.md` §Scope; exclude generated / vendored files. **Held-doc carve-out (do NOT raise THIS finding):** a doc comment the purge deliberately HELD - because the build-safety pre-check found a docs-required-at-error signal on that member (.NET GenerateDocumentationFile + CS1591-as-error, Rust `#![deny(missing_docs)]`, Java javadoc `-Werror`, ESLint require-jsdoc, pydocstyle/ruff D), or a degraded-mode / no-response HOLD applied to a multi-line load-bearing why - is OUT of scope for this slug; a correctly-held doc is not a `comment-hygiene-purge-survivor` miss. This carve-out narrows ONLY this slug; `comment-necessity` (which governs NEW / rewritten comments and already excludes pre-existing comments) is unaffected. A degraded-mode / no-response HOLD never covers restatement / narration - that is deleted unconditionally and stays a survivor if any escaped. Per-site discriminator: a narrative doc summary on a member subject to a docs-required-at-error build is NOT a survivor finding, whereas a narrative summary on an `internal` / `private` / unexported member, or on a public member with NO docs-required signal, IS. **Acceptable (do NOT flag):** functional / tool-consumed comments (shebangs, build / codegen directives, lint / suppression directives, runtime-consumed docstrings, copyright / license, THROWAWAY headers); a single terse load-bearing why; a held doc per the carve-out above. |  | MEDIUM |
| strongly-typed-id-preference | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW declaration in the diff that introduces a primitive (`Guid`, `long`, `int`, `string`, `uint`, `ulong`, `short`, `ushort`) as an ID surface, in ANY of these positions: (i) field/property/parameter/local whose name ends with `Id`/`ID`/`id`; (ii) generic type argument that semantically represents an ID (e.g., `Dictionary<long, ModalSession>` where the `long` key is a modal ID; `HashSet<Guid>` of filter IDs; `IDictionary<TKey, TValue>` instantiations where `TKey` is a domain identifier): check whether a strongly-typed ID wrapper (`public readonly record struct XxxId(Guid Value)` or equivalent) already exists in the codebase for this domain. If a wrapper exists, USE IT. If no wrapper exists and this introduces a NEW logical ID domain, prefer creating one following the established codebase pattern (factory `public static XxxId Create() => new(Guid.NewGuid());`). **Acceptable primitives (do NOT flag)**: extensions of an existing primitive-ID subsystem where the surrounding public surface already uses primitives AND switching one site would create inconsistency (the reviewer MUST cite the specific pre-existing primitive ID surface to invoke this carve-out - dodge-by-assertion is not sufficient); EF Core entity row IDs that match the database schema (`long Id`, `int Id`); framework-required types (Blazor `[Parameter] public string Id { get; set; }`, `[Parameter] public Guid SelectorId { get; set; }`); Windows platform IDs (`ushort Id`, `int Id` per platform conventions); interop / P/Invoke handles; correlation/trace/request/diagnostic tokens scoped to single-operation tracing, where the value never crosses domain boundaries. Use the diff to distinguish NEW IDs from extensions of existing primitive-ID code; baseline `long Id` properties on entities are NOT in scope. |  |
| prefer-async-suffix | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW sync call added in the diff INSIDE a genuinely async method - defined as: the enclosing method declaration has the `async` keyword OR the method body contains `await` somewhere (this excludes sync bridge/adapter methods that merely return `Task`/`Task<T>`/`ValueTask`/`ValueTask<T>` via `Task.CompletedTask`, `Task.FromResult`, or by returning the result of another sync call): verify whether the receiver type has a `<Method>Async` sibling. If yes, prefer the async overload. **If choosing sync where Async exists**, the call MUST be commented with the reason (e.g., `// sync bridge: awaiting captures SynchronizationContext`, `// sync OK: caller holds non-async lock`). Unjustified sync-when-Async-exists is a flag-able violation regardless of the rest of the diff. **High-signal sites to check**: `DbConnection.Open()` / `SqliteConnection.Open()` / `SqlConnection.Open()` → `OpenAsync()` (note: `IDbConnection` itself does NOT define `OpenAsync` - only the concrete/abstract `DbConnection` hierarchy does); `DbContext.SaveChanges()` → `SaveChangesAsync()` (also caught by `async-correctness` rg - DO NOT duplicate-flag if `async-correctness` already surfaced it); `Stream.Dispose()` → `await DisposeAsync()`; `Stream.Read/Write/Flush()` → `Async` versions; `CancellationTokenRegistration.Dispose()` → `DisposeAsync()`; `HttpClient.Send(HttpRequestMessage)` → `SendAsync()` (note: most `HttpClient.*` methods are already `Async`-only - only `Send` is the sync one); `IServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<IDbContextFactory<T>>().CreateDbContext()` → `CreateDbContextAsync()`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: types whose API has no `Async` sibling (`Utf8JsonReader.Read`, `Process.Start`, `ILogger.Debug`, `Math.Sqrt`); sync calls in non-async contexts (`Dispose()` on a `using` statement at end of a sync method - `using` itself handles the dispose form); sync bridge/adapter implementations of an async-shaped contract (e.g., `public Task SaveAsync() { _writer.Flush(); return Task.CompletedTask; }`); explicit diagnostic-only synchronous I/O where the cost is intentional and commented; sites already surfaced by `async-correctness` rg battery - do NOT duplicate-flag. Use the diff to find NEW sync calls in async contexts; do NOT flag pre-existing sync calls in unchanged code. |  | HIGH |
| publication-barrier-order | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified writer method in the diff that updates multiple fields under a single lock AND publishes one of them via `Volatile.Write` / `Interlocked.Exchange` / `Interlocked.CompareExchange` as a "publication barrier" intended to make the rest visible to cross-thread readers: verify the volatile/interlocked write happens LAST in the write sequence (so it acts as the publication point), NOT first or in the middle. The .NET memory model guarantees writes preceding a `Volatile.Write` (or full-fence `Interlocked` op) are not reordered past it - so any cross-thread reader that observes the volatile-published value is guaranteed to also see the prior writes. If the volatile-write is first/middle, plain-field writes can be reordered past it from a CROSS-THREAD reader's perspective (the lock body's release barrier only fires on `Monitor.Exit`, but cross-thread readers using a DIFFERENT lock or no lock can observe the partial state during the writer's lock body). **High-signal patterns to check**: `Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, newId); ActiveType = newType;` (id published BEFORE state) - wrong; should be `ActiveType = newType; Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, newId);` (id LAST). Mirror for clear paths: clear plain fields first, then volatile-write sentinel id LAST. Also flag `_idCounter++; Volatile.Write(ref _activeId, _idCounter);` followed by plain writes - same issue, reorder so id-write is last. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: lock-protected reads via the SAME lock as the writer (mutual exclusion makes ordering irrelevant); writer-side-only state with no cross-thread readers; explicit `Interlocked.MemoryBarrier()` / `Thread.MemoryBarrier()` between writes (rare, only when the volatile-write semantics aren't sufficient). Apply to writer paths only; reader-side double-read patterns (re-read the volatile after plain reads) are a complementary concern, not this rule. |  |
| event-ordering-test-subscription | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW test in the diff that asserts ORDERING between an EVENT (e.g., `StateChanged?.Invoke()`, `PropertyChanged`, `INotifyPropertyChanged.PropertyChanged`) and a SUBJECT-ACTION (e.g., a TCS completion that resumes an awaiter, a state transition observable by the consumer): verify the test's event subscription happens AFTER any non-asserted setup action that fires the same event. Subscribing BEFORE a setup `Show()`/`Initialize()`/`Open()`/`Start()` that ALSO fires the event causes the SETUP event to set the assertion flag, masking whether the SUBJECT-ACTION-fired event actually preceded the awaiter. The test then passes regardless of correctness - a false-positive risk that defeats the test's purpose. **High-signal pattern**: ``sut.StateChanged += () => { if (!awaiterResumed) flag = true; }; sut.Show<...>(); ... sut.CancelActive();`` - the `Show` event sets `flag=true` before `CancelActive` runs. **Fix options**: (a) move `+= ...` AFTER the setup `Show()`, (b) count event invocations and assert on the Nth, (c) reset the flag between setup and subject-action. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: tests where the event is wired up via DI/constructor and there's no separable setup-vs-subject phase; tests asserting "event fires at least once" rather than "event fires before X"; tests where the setup-fired event and the subject-fired event are distinguishable by some other property (e.g., the event argument carries identity). Use the diff to find NEW tests; pre-existing test patterns with the same shape are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| event-completion-fault-isolation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified writer method in the diff that BOTH (a) invokes a public event (e.g., `StateChanged?.Invoke()`, `PropertyChanged?.Invoke(...)`, custom multi-cast delegate) OR a caller-supplied callback (`Action`, `Func<T>`, `Action<T>`, custom delegate parameter) AND (b) subsequently completes a `TaskCompletionSource`, invokes a captured cancel delegate, or otherwise resumes an awaiting caller: verify the post-event completion is wrapped in `try/finally` so the completion runs even when the subscriber/callback throws. Pattern that BREAKS: ``StateChanged?.Invoke(); tcs.TrySetResult(value);`` - if any subscriber throws, the TCS never completes and the awaiter hangs forever. Same hang risk with caller callbacks: ``onComplete?.Invoke(); tcs.TrySetResult(value);``. Pattern that WORKS: ``try { StateChanged?.Invoke(); } finally { tcs.TrySetResult(value); }`` - TCS completion is guaranteed regardless of subscriber exceptions; the exception still propagates to the caller for diagnostics. This applies to ALL completion shapes: `TrySetResult`, `TrySetCanceled`, `TrySetException`, `cancelDelegate?.Invoke()` capturing a TCS, `IPromise.Resolve`, etc. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: event/callback invocations with no follow-up completion (purely notification); event invocations where the completion semantically depends on subscriber outcome (rare; document if so); event/callback invocations that follow rather than precede completion (e.g., `tcs.TrySetResult(value); StateChanged?.Invoke();` - but verify this ordering is intentional, since it can violate the publication-before-resume discipline that subscribers often rely on). Use the diff to find NEW writer paths; pre-existing event-then-complete patterns in unchanged code are not in scope. |  | HIGH |
| test-aaa-pattern | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW test method (`[Fact]` or `[Theory]`) added in the diff: verify the test body is structured with `// Arrange`, `// Act`, and `// Assert` markers separating the three phases (the AAA pattern). This convention is enforced across the codebase's existing test files and improves readability + reviewability. **Apply when**: a test has a discernible setup → action → verification flow. The markers must be on their own lines as comments (e.g., `        // Arrange`); inline comments don't count. **Acceptable variations (do NOT flag)**: tests with no Act phase (pure state assertions on initial construction) may use `// Arrange` + `// Assert` only - drop `// Act` rather than insert a placeholder; tests that mix Act+Assert (e.g., `Assert.Throws<T>(() => sut.Method())`) may combine via a single `// Act + Assert` marker; data-only `[Theory]` rows that exercise multiple cases in a loop may use the markers around the loop, not within. **NOT flagged**: pre-existing tests in modified files that lacked AAA before this PR (not in scope - AAA-converting baseline tests is separate cleanup work); micro-benchmarks; helper test methods that aren't `[Fact]`/`[Theory]`; generated/source-generated test files (files with `<auto-generated>` headers, files inside `obj/` or generator output directories, or files where the generator owns the layout - adding AAA markers to generator output is not a valid remediation). Use the diff to find NEW hand-authored tests; mirror the existing codebase convention. |  |
| panel-artifact-leakage | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\\b(Slot \\d+\|R\\d+ (finding\|fix\|round\|rework\|ready)\|PR \\d+\\+\\d+\|pre-(implementation\|PR-creation) panel\|round[ -]?\\d+\|\\bbots?\\b.*(caught\|finding\|flagged))\\b","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  | HIGH |
| resource-cleanup | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"new\\s+(CancellationTokenSource\|SqliteConnection\|SqlConnection\|FileStream\|StreamReader\|StreamWriter\|HttpClient\|ProcessStartInfo)\\(","glob":["*.cs"]},"diff":{"pattern":"new\\s+(CancellationTokenSource\|SqliteConnection\|SqlConnection\|FileStream\|StreamReader\|StreamWriter\|HttpClient\|ProcessStartInfo)\\(","glob":["*.cs"]}} |  |  |
| naming-convention | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"^\\s+var [A-Z][a-z]","glob":["tests/**/*.cs"]} |  |  |
| async-correctness | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":"SaveChanges\\(\\)\|_ = InvokeAsync\|\\.Result\\b\|\\.Wait\\(\\)","glob":["*.cs"]},"diff":{"pattern":"SaveChanges\\(\\)\|_ = InvokeAsync\|\\.Result\\b\|\\.Wait\\(\\)","glob":["*.cs"]}} |  |  |
| thread-safety | tree-scoped | {"pattern":"IProgress<.*>\|new Progress<","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| bounds-empty-collection | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\\b\\w+\\[0\\]\|stackalloc \\w+\\[\\w+\\.Length\\]","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| aria-binding | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"aria-\|_is[A-Z]\\w+\\s*=\\s*(true\|false\|!_)","glob":["*.razor","*.razor.cs"]} |  |  |
| regex-validation | tree-scoped | {"pattern":"new Regex\\(\|filter.*regex\|regex.*filter","glob":["*.cs","*.razor"]} |  |  |
| razor-binding | hybrid | {"tree":{"pattern":":empty","glob":["*.css"]},"diff":{"pattern":"@onscroll\|@onkeydown\|@onkeyup\|(public\|protected) (async )?(void\|Task) (Show\|Hide\|Toggle\|Open\|Close)\\w*","glob":["*.razor","*.razor.cs"]}} |  |  |
| null-handling | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"\"\\[.*\\{[^}]*\\}\\]\"","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| dead-code-unused | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.cs` / `.ts` / `.tsx` / `.js` / `.jsx` file in the diff: identify symbols introduced by the PR that have no incoming reference. Common cases: a private method called by only one site that the same PR removes (the method is now dead); a parameter added to a method that the body never reads; a local variable assigned but never observed; an enum value with no producer or consumer; an import/using statement added for a type the PR no longer references; a removed `if` branch that leaves a captured local unused. Flag the dead symbol and propose deletion. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: public API surface (callers may live in other repos / out-of-tree consumers); test-only helpers added in the same PR that the test file itself uses; symbols guarded behind `[Conditional("DEBUG")]` / `#if` directives; designer-generated members; framework conventions that look unused but are reflected over (e.g., Blazor `[Parameter]`, Fluxor `[FeatureState]`, EF Core navigation properties, `[JsonPropertyName]`-decorated DTOs, MSTest/xUnit attribute-discovered methods). Use the diff to find the NEW symbols; do not regress on legacy unused code that predates this PR. |  |
| null-dereference-risk | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW receiver-access (`obj.Member`, `obj[index]`, `obj()`, `obj!.Member`, optional chaining `obj?.Member.X` where `.X` is non-null-safe) added in the diff: verify the receiver cannot be `null` at that program point. Specifically: track null-conditional propagation (`a?.B.C` - if `a` is null then `.B` is null then `.C` NREs), parameter origins for nullable-annotated parameters consumed unguarded, fields declared without initializer that can race null-default with init, return-value chains where an upstream method can return null. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: receivers already guarded by `if (x is null) return` / `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(x)` / `Debug.Assert(x is not null)` immediately above; non-nullable annotated locals/parameters with no flow that could produce null (full compiler NRT analysis suffices - do not double-flag what the compiler already enforces); `!` null-forgiving operator on a receiver where the surrounding code has just type-tested or pattern-matched. Use the diff to identify NEW receiver accesses; pre-existing potential-null patterns in unchanged code are not in scope. |  |
| code-comment-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or hunk-adjacent inline/block code comment or config-file comment (`//`, `/* */`, `.csproj`/`.props`/MSBuild config comments) in or next to modified lines: verify the comment text matches what the adjacent code, build/config transform, or platform API actually does. Distinct from `doc-impl-mismatch`: that rule owns ALL XML doc comments and prose claims (claim falsification); THIS rule owns inline code comments and config-file comments whose text drifted from the adjacent code/config through a refactor or edit. Do not duplicate-flag if an owning rule already surfaced the issue. **High-signal patterns**: `// Create TestButton` above `var x = new Label("TestLabel");` (creates Label, not Button); `// Deadlock on iOS .NET 10` above code that does not exhibit that deadlock; `// Cancel and dispose` above a method body that no longer disposes; `// Returns null when X` above a method whose new body always returns a default; `// 5 seconds` above `TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(3000)`. **Extraction-drift sub-case** (frequently missed): when a helper method (or interface, or class) is EXTRACTED from a larger method in the diff, inline/block code comments that traveled with the extracted code (a drifted XML doc summary on the extracted member is `doc-impl-mismatch`) often describe operations the new location does NOT perform - e.g., a catch-block comment saying "torn down between the _disposed check and the dispatcher call" preserved into an extracted helper whose body has only the dispatcher call (no preceding _disposed check). For every NEW method body whose code matches a pattern that previously lived in a larger method (extraction signature: new private/internal method whose body is a shorter recombination of code from another method in the diff), audit each comment for references to variables, local guards, or sibling operations that aren't present in the extracted scope. Flag every drift. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: comments that describe *general intent* and remain accurate even if implementation detail changes (e.g., `// Compute the running total` above any aggregate); comments explaining a non-obvious *why* (concurrency invariant, race-safety) that the code still upholds; comments referencing a spec/standard that the code conforms to. Use the diff to identify comments whose adjacent code/config changed; pre-existing comments far from untouched code are not in scope, but unchanged comments inside the modified hunk or immediately explaining a changed line are in scope. |  | HIGH |

| sqlite-transactional-coupling-for-dependent-statements | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or MODIFIED method in the diff that issues TWO OR MORE SQL statements on the same database connection where the SECOND statement's correctness DEPENDS on the FIRST's effect: verify the dependent statements are wrapped in an explicit transaction with the appropriate isolation level. Without a transaction, each statement auto-commits and another connection's writes can land in the gap, breaking the temporal coupling. **High-signal patterns**: (a) collision-lookup after `INSERT OR IGNORE` / `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` (zero rows affected → SELECT to fetch the existing row; another writer's DELETE in the gap surfaces as "row vanished"); (b) read-then-update where the UPDATE's WHERE-clause assumes the row's state from the SELECT (a concurrent writer can change the row between read and update); (c) lookup-then-delete where the DELETE's WHERE-clause depends on the lookup result (lost-update class); (d) check-then-act balance / counter / cursor operations. **Required for `Microsoft.Data.Sqlite`**: `using var tx = connection.BeginTransaction(IsolationLevel.Serializable);` (maps to `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` - acquires a RESERVED write lock for the transaction lifetime). Set `cmd.Transaction = tx;` on each command + `tx.Commit()` on success; throw before commit if invariant violated. **For other ADO.NET providers** (`SqlConnection`, `NpgsqlConnection`, `MySqlConnection`): use the provider-specific equivalent (`BeginTransaction(IsolationLevel.Serializable)` for SQL Server/PostgreSQL; `BeginTransaction(IsolationLevel.Serializable)` for MySQL but verify provider docs for lock-acquisition timing). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: single-statement methods (no temporal coupling possible); SELECT followed by UPDATE/DELETE whose WHERE-clause is self-validating (e.g., `WHERE id=$id AND row_version=$known_version` - concurrent writer's update would change the version and the UPDATE would affect 0 rows, observable to caller); fully idempotent compound patterns where the second statement is correct regardless of concurrent writers (e.g., `INSERT OR REPLACE` based on a known key); BCL synchronization primitives (`lock` / `SemaphoreSlim`) that serialize ALL callers across all connections. (panel-miss): `AddOrReturnExistingFilter` issued `INSERT OR IGNORE` then a tuple-lookup `SELECT` in the collision branch on the same connection without an enclosing transaction; another connection's prune-delete between the two statements would have surfaced as `InvalidOperationException` ("INSERT OR IGNORE collided but no existing row matched the tuple"). The publish-gate panel framing focused on the UNIQUE INDEX semantics and missed the temporal-coupling concern. Fix: wrap both statements in `BeginTransaction(IsolationLevel.Serializable)`. |  |  |

Or extract an `UpsertById<T>` extension on `ImmutableList<T>` used by both reducer and effect. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: snapshots built for read-only display (no downstream destructive logic); snapshots where the dispatched success action's reducer is NOT idempotent (e.g., `.Add(x)` only, no FindIndex+SetItem guard - the snapshot then accurately reflects post-reducer state); effects that do NOT dispatch a subsequent success action mutating the same collection; effects that construct the snapshot AFTER the dispatch by re-reading `state.Value.<collection>` (the reducer's commit is naturally picked up); UI thread effects in single-threaded dispatchers where concurrent dispatches are impossible by construction (cite the dispatcher contract). **Required verification**: read the paired reducer for the dispatched success action. (panel-miss): `HandleItemFilterApplied` collision branch built `entries.Add(collisionEntry)` snapshot for the downstream `PruneFromSnapshot` helper, but the paired `ReduceAddEntrySuccess` reducer is idempotent via FindIndex+SetItem. Slot 5 verified the branch in panel iter 2 and explicitly reasoned about ONE dispatch ordering ("original inserter's AddSuccessAction may not have committed") while overlooking the other ("may have already committed") - also covered by the *Adversarial dispatch-ordering enumeration* discipline in `pr-creation-mirror-prompt.md`. |  |  |
