Fix: change return type to `Task` + `await` the inner call. Applies regardless of method parameter count. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: lambdas that invoke purely-synchronous methods (`@onclick="@(() => _counter++)"`); lambdas wrapping `Task`-returning calls with an explicit fire-and-forget pattern documented inline (e.g., `// fire-and-forget: progress reporter is non-failing by contract`); EventCallback assignments without a lambda wrapper. **Audit method**: regex-grep `@on\w+\s*=\s*"@?\(\s*(\(\s*[^)]*\)\|[a-z_]\w*)\s*=>\s*[^"]*\.InvokeAsync\(` AND `@on\w+\s*=\s*"@?\(\s*(\(\s*[^)]*\)\|[a-z_]\w*)\s*=>\s*\{[^}]*InvokeAsync\(` in all NEW or modified `.razor` files; also scan `.razor.cs` files for `(private\|protected\|internal\|public)\s+void\s+On\w+\w*\s*\(\s*[^)]*\)\s*=>\s*\w+\.InvokeAsync\(` and `(private\|protected\|internal\|public)\s+void\s+On\w+\w*\s*\(\s*[^)]*\)\s*\{[^}]*InvokeAsync\(`. Covers parameterless `()`, single-named-param `e`, single-typed-param `(MouseEventArgs e)`, and any code-behind handler signature. For each match, verify the lambda is `async` OR the wrapper returns `Task` + `await`s the inner call. |  | HIGH |
| shared-helper-overwrites-caller-scoped-state | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified method in the diff that is invoked from MORE THAN ONE call site (single-row path + bulk-path; single-entity path + collection-path; per-item path + batch path) AND that mutates state owned by only ONE of the calling paths (e.g., a bulk-selection set, a per-batch staging buffer, a global "pending" flag): verify the state mutation is either (a) scoped to the subset of state the call actually relates to (e.g., remove only the affected file names from a selection set, not the entire set), OR (b) conditioned on a calling-path flag passed in by the caller, OR (c) moved out of the shared helper into the bulk-only caller. **High-signal failure**: `RemoveItemsAsync(IReadOnlyList<string> fileNames, ...)` unconditionally calls `_selectedForRemoval.Clear()` at the end. The bulk path (`OnBulkRemoveClickAsync`) populates `_selectedForRemoval` then calls this method; the per-row Remove path (`RemoveItem(entry)`) passes a single file name then ALSO calls this same method. Result: if user has selected {A, B} via checkboxes for bulk removal, then clicks the per-row trash on unrelated file X, the X removal completes AND silently wipes the A/B selection - surprising and destructive. **Fix patterns**: (a) scope the clear - `foreach (var name in fileNames) { _selectedForRemoval.Remove(name); }` (only removes the names actually processed); (b) condition the clear - add a `bool clearBulkSelection` parameter passed `true` by the bulk caller and `false` by the per-row caller; (c) move the clear out of the shared helper into the bulk caller - `OnBulkRemoveClickAsync` clears `_selectedForRemoval` AFTER calling `RemoveItemsAsync`. The orphan-pruning path in `OnDatabaseEntriesChanged` (which removes entries from `_selectedForRemoval` when their underlying entry disappears) already handles the natural cleanup; the unconditional clear is redundant AND destructive. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: shared methods that explicitly document "clears all caller state on success" in XML doc (cite the contract); shared methods whose state-clear is semantically required by ALL callers (e.g., a method that publishes a finalized transaction MUST clear the pending-transaction buffer regardless of caller); private helpers invoked from one site (no call-site divergence to worry about). **Audit method**: identify private/internal methods in the diff that (a) have ≥2 call sites in the same class, (b) mutate instance state OTHER than the parameter values, (c) the mutated state is observed by sibling methods. For each, verify the mutation is either subset-scoped (only touches state related to the parameters) or conditioned on a calling-path indicator. |  |
| guard-predicate-incomplete-after-multi-signal-redesign | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified click-guard / disable-predicate / aria-disabled expression / early-return condition in the diff that gates a destructive or state-mutating action AND the surrounding feature has been redesigned to surface state via MULTIPLE signals (e.g., a coordinator's flag PLUS a per-row progress entry from a banner service; a primary IsLoading flag PLUS a per-item ProgressTracker; a session-scoped boolean PLUS an entity-scoped collection): verify the guard checks ALL relevant signals, not just one. A guard that only checks the original primary signal will permit the action when ONLY the secondary signal is set - which is precisely the case that the multi-signal redesign was meant to handle. **High-signal failure**: `OrderRow.razor` `ActionKind.RestoreFromBackup` button uses `@onclick="@(async () => { if (!IsActionBlocked) { await OnRestoreFromBackup.InvokeAsync(); } })"`. `IsActionBlocked` reflects the session-wide `Coordinator.IsAnyUpgradeInFlight` flag - but Background-scope upgrades from import bypass the Coordinator and only show via `UpgradeProgress` (per-row banner-service signal). So when an import-triggered upgrade is in flight for the SAME database that the user clicks Restore on, `IsActionBlocked` is false → restore proceeds during an active upgrade → file-in-use / corruption. The fix sibling for Remove already does dual-signal detection via `IsAnyFileUpgrading(...)`. The Restore guard needs the same. **Required check pattern**: any guard predicate whose feature has dual-signal detection elsewhere in the SAME component (or in a sibling action's guard) MUST mirror the multi-signal check. **Fix patterns**: (a) extract a property like `private bool IsRestoreBlocked => IsActionBlocked \|\| UpgradeProgress is not null;` (or `is { CurrentEntryName: var n } && string.Equals(n, Entry.FileName, ...)` for per-row specificity) and reuse for both the aria-disabled rendering AND the click guard; (b) widen the existing `IsActionBlocked` property to include all relevant signals (riskier - may affect other call sites that intentionally check only the coordinator). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: guards where the secondary signal is provably equivalent to the primary signal (the redesign ensured both flip together); guards whose action is not affected by the secondary signal (e.g., a "Settings"-only action doesn't care about per-row progress); single-signal features (no redesign that introduced multiple signals). **Audit method**: when reviewing a `.razor` file with multiple action buttons (`case ActionKind.X:` switch arms), compile a list of distinct `aria-disabled`/`@onclick`-guard predicates used per arm. If two arms gate the same underlying concept (e.g., "while upgrading") with DIFFERENT predicates, the one with the narrower predicate is suspect - propose the wider one. Cross-check the dual-signal detection helpers (e.g., `IsAnyFileUpgrading`, `GetUpgradeProgressForEntry`) that exist in the parent component; if they exist, the row-level guards should be consistent. |  | HIGH |
| user-facing-message-content-regression-after-callback-extraction | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified call site in the diff that REPLACES a caller-supplied delegate (`Func<bool, ...> confirmAsync`, `Action<DialogContext>`, etc.) with a static/no-op delegate (`(_, _) => Task.FromResult(true)`, `_ => { }`) AND the previous delegate consumed a CONTEXT FLAG passed by the producer (e.g., `showCloseReopenWarning`, `isHighRisk`, `hasUnsavedChanges`): verify the UI surface that previously rendered the context-dependent message still renders an equivalent message. A static delegate silently drops the context flag → the producer's context-aware rendering is lost without any compile-time signal. **High-signal failure**: previous flow used `confirmRemoveAsync(showCloseReopenWarning, cancellationToken)` where the UI implementation rendered `"{fileName} is currently enabled. Removing will close and reopen any affected log views. Are you sure?"` when `showCloseReopenWarning=true`. New flow uses `Coordinator.RemoveItemAsync(fileName, static (_, _) => Task.FromResult(true))` and `BuildPlainRemoveMessage` always returns the generic `"Are you sure you want to remove {fileName}?"` regardless of state. Users lose information they previously relied on before confirming a destructive action that closes/reopens their logs. **Fix patterns**: (a) precompute the context flag in the new caller (inspect entity state + inject the dependency that the old callback used to consume - e.g., `IReloadCoordinator.HasActiveItems`) AND fold the warning into the message-building helper (`BuildPlainRemoveMessage(fileNames, willCloseAndReopen)`); (b) keep the callback-with-context shape but pass the same predicate through; (c) split the new message-building helper into named overloads (`BuildSimpleRemoveMessage`, `BuildRemoveWithReloadWarningMessage`). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: callback replacements where the previous context flag was unused / never set; callback replacements where the producer's message already incorporates the same context (no UI loss). **Audit method**: when reviewing a callback replacement of shape `(_, _) => Task.FromResult(true)` (or any other static delegate), `git log -p -S "<previous delegate name>"` to find the previous IMPLEMENTATION. Read the implementation's body; identify every parameter consumed and every context-dependent branch in the previous rendering. Verify the new flow preserves each branch's message OR explicitly documents why it's dropped. |  | HIGH |
| failure-detail-collected-but-not-surfaced | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified method in the diff that collects per-item failure information into a list / dictionary (`List<(string, string)> failed`, `Dictionary<string, Exception> errors`, `IReadOnlyList<Failure> rejected`, `List<Failure> failures`, `Dictionary<string, string> skipped`) inside a loop AND announces ONLY the count (or the count + label) to a user-visible surface: verify the failure DETAIL (the captured reason / exception message / context) reaches at least ONE operator-diagnostic sink (TraceLogger, ILogger, structured-logging Property) AND/OR is surfaced to the user in a follow-up inline alert / error banner / detail dialog. Silently collected but never-rendered failure details lose operator diagnostics AND the user's ability to act on the failure (retry the specific failed item, file a bug with the exact error). **High-signal failure**: `RemoveItemsAsync` collects `failed.Add((fileName, ex.Message))` for per-file failures but only announces `$"{failed.Count} removal{(failed.Count == 1 ? "" : "s")} failed."`. The `(fileName, ex.Message)` tuple is dropped on function exit. **Fix patterns**: (a) log each failure via `TraceLogger.Warning($"... failed: {tuple.FileName}: {tuple.Reason}")` BEFORE announcing the count (operator gets stack-traced detail); (b) include the first failure's detail in the user-facing announcement (`$"{failed.Count} removals failed. First: {failed[0].FileName}: {failed[0].Reason}"`); (c) post a follow-up inline alert / error banner with the full list of failures (user-facing detail, actionable retry). At minimum, option (a) is required so failures aren't invisible to operators investigating "user reports removal didn't work but no error message". **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: cases where the underlying coordinator already routes each failure to an error banner / log sink internally (verify by inspecting the coordinator's failure path); fire-and-forget batch operations whose contract documents "best-effort; failures are silent"; failures whose reason field is always identical to the count-summary (rare). **Audit method**: regex-grep `(failed\|errors\|rejected\|failures\|skipped\|warnings)\.Add\(` for any collection-of-failures pattern in NEW code; ALSO grep for `Dictionary<.*,\s*(Exception\|string)>` declarations naming a failures-collection; ALSO grep for `List<\([^)]*\)>` tuple-list declarations naming a failures-collection. For each match, trace whether the collected detail reaches a TraceLogger / banner / inline-alert call before function exit. If not, flag. |  | HIGH |
| post-refactor-state-tracking-loss-of-sticky-flag | review-pass-only | {} | For every refactor / extraction in the diff that REPLACES a previously-monolithic UI component's state-tracking with a derived signal (e.g., session-scoped `_settingsDirty` flag replaced with `HasDatabaseStateChanged => initialSet.SetEquals(currentSet)`): verify the new derived signal STILL covers every state-change path the old flag tracked. Refactors that narrow the tracking surface to a derived property often miss orthogonal state changes (e.g., content overwrites that don't change a filename set) that were transparently caught by the old sticky-flag-on-any-mutation approach. **High-signal failure**: `ManageItemsTab` extracted from Settings. Old Settings flow had a sticky `_anyDatabaseChange` flag set on import/upgrade/remove/restore - so closing the modal after any DB activity triggered the reload prompt. New `HasDatabaseStateChanged` is derived from `_initialActiveSnapshot.SetEquals(ComputeActiveSet())` + sticky `_schemaUpgradeOccurred` / `_restorationOccurred`. **Missing path**: `ImportDatabase` succeeds → if the imported file overwrites an EXISTING enabled DB, the active filename set is unchanged so the diff is false, AND none of the sticky flags are set on import. Result: close-modal skips reload prompt; user's open log views silently see stale data from the pre-overwrite DB. **Fix patterns**: (a) restore the sticky-flag approach for the missing path (`if (outcome.DatabaseStateChanged) { _schemaUpgradeOccurred = true; }`); (b) make the derived signal cover the missing path explicitly (track content-version hash per file in the snapshot instead of just filenames); (c) propagate richer outcome metadata up from the coordinator (`ImportOutcome.OverwrittenFileNames`) and check overlap with the active set. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: refactors where the new derived signal demonstrably covers ALL old paths (cite the proof: each old sticky-flag set-site has a corresponding signal in the new derivation); refactors where the lost path is intentionally retired (cite the design doc). **Audit method**: when reviewing a refactor that replaces a sticky-flag with a derived property, list every set-site of the old flag (`git log -p -S "_oldFlag = true"`). For each set-site, identify the input that triggered the flag (event, callback, method call). Verify the new derived property reads that same input OR a strictly-larger superset. If any set-site's input is NOT covered by the new derivation, flag the omission. **Generalization**: applies to ANY sticky-flag → derived-signal refactor (UI, services, coordinators) - not just Razor components. The phrase "sticky flag" means any boolean set ONCE per session-meaningful event and never reset; replacing it with a snapshot-equality derivation requires every original set-site to map to an input the derivation reads. |  | HIGH |
| parent-prop-narrowing-occludes-component-wide-signal | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified parent-to-child component parameter binding in the diff that PASSES a NARROWED version of a component/session-wide signal (e.g., `IsActionBlocked="@(IsActionBlocked && !Coordinator.IsUpgradeInFlight(entry.FileName))"` - wide flag AND-ed with a per-item exclusion): verify the child component CAN still make accurate guard decisions for cross-cutting concerns when the narrowed signal is false. The narrowing is usually intended to enable a per-item action that the wide flag would block (e.g., a Cancel button on the row currently upgrading), but it inadvertently disables the child's ability to render any OTHER cross-cutting action as blocked on that same row. Applies to ALL component models - Razor / Blazor parent→child params, React props, Vue props, custom XAML data-context narrowing - anywhere a parent composes a logical mask of a wider signal before passing to a child. **High-signal failure**: `ManageItemsTab.razor:34` passes `IsActionBlocked="@(IsActionBlocked && !Coordinator.IsUpgradeInFlight(entry.FileName))"`. For the row whose file IS currently upgrading, this evaluates to FALSE. But `OrderRow.PrimaryAction` prioritizes `BackupExists → RestoreFromBackup` over `IsUpgrading → Spinner`, so a recovery row for the in-flight file renders an enabled-looking Restore button - even though `RestoreFromBackup` server-side refuses while any upgrade is active. The user sees no aria-disabled signal, clicks, and sees a refusal toast instead of an upfront disabled state. **Fix patterns**: (a) pass the wide signal unmodified (`IsActionBlocked="@IsActionBlocked"`) and let the child compose its own per-action predicate that combines wide + per-row state; (b) introduce a separate per-action narrowing parameter (`IsUpgradeAllowedForThisRow="..."`) so the wide signal stays accurate AND the per-row override is explicit; (c) move the narrowing logic INTO the child component (parent passes both signals; child decides). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: narrowing where the child has NO cross-cutting action that depends on the un-narrowed signal (verify by inspecting every guard in the child); narrowing where the child explicitly receives BOTH the wide AND per-row signals separately. **Audit method**: when reviewing a parent-to-child parameter binding that contains `&& !`, `\|\|`, or any other logical composition narrowing a wider flag, list every action / guard / disabled-predicate in the child. For each, verify the action is NOT affected by the un-narrowed signal - OR add a separate parameter for the un-narrowed signal. **Note**: previously named `parent-prop-narrowing-occludes-modal-wide-signal`; renamed for project-agnosticism (rule applies beyond modals to any parent-child composition). |  | HIGH |
| stacked-pr-fix-propagation-gap-during-rebase | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** For every stack of PRs (foundation → polish → feature) where a defect found in the foundation PR is fixed on a HIGHER PR in the stack via a fix commit that is NOT cherry-picked back down to the foundation: when the foundation PR is reviewed again, the same defect will be flagged because the foundation tree does not contain the fix. The fix must be applied to the LOWEST PR in the stack whose tree contains the defect, then rebased upward. **Why this matters**: stacked PRs share file lineage. Fixing a defect on the upper PR's branch (because that's where the agent is currently working) creates a "fix-propagation gap" - the foundation PR still ships the bug, the bot re-flags it on the next review, and the agent has to fix it twice. This is especially common when the bot reviews the foundation PR in a later round AFTER the agent has already addressed the same defect on the upper PR's branch (the agent's mental model is "fixed in PR X, done", but each PR is reviewed against its OWN tree). **High-signal failure**: a foundation PR's tree included a commit that added two buttons with block-lambda click handlers; the bot flagged the unawaited lambdas on the foundation PR. The agent's fix commit landed on a HIGHER PR's branch (where it was iterating, top-down) instead of the foundation branch. When the foundation PR was reviewed again, the bot still saw the unfixed lambdas in the foundation's own tree, because each PR is reviewed against its OWN tree. **Required workflow**: (1) when classifying a bot finding, identify the LOWEST PR in the stack whose tree contains the defective code (`git log <branch> -- <file>` to find the introducing commit, then map commit → PR); (2) apply the fix to THAT PR's branch; (3) rebase upper PRs onto the fix; (4) push all affected branches. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: fixes that are scope-locked to a specific upper PR (e.g., a fix that only applies to a feature added in the upper PR - not present in the foundation tree); cases where the lower PR was already merged before the finding surfaced. **Audit method**: every fix commit on a stacked-PR branch should be paired with a check via `git log <foundation-branch> -- <file>` to confirm the defect is NOT also present in the lower branch's tree; if it is, the fix must land on the lower branch first then rebase. |  | HIGH |

| implementation-to-commit-transition-bypasses-user-approval | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** At the implementation → git transition (tests green, lint clean, optionally PR-quality-gate `core_rules_acknowledged` block emitted), the agent's FIRST action MUST be mechanical classification (`git status --porcelain`) - the agent NEVER auto-stages code (`git add .` / `-A` / `--all` forbidden). Under the inverted staging model (`AGENTS.md` §0 + `pre-commit.md`), the USER stages reviewed code (the review-scope signal); the agent commits ONLY the user's staged set, and the §0 `git commit` `ask_user` - which MUST print the enumerated staged set (user-code vs gate-artifacts, flagged unexpected paths) - is the human sign-off. PR-quality-gate ack passing does NOT satisfy the §0 user-approval gate; the two gates are DISTINCT (see `AGENTS.md` §0 dual-gate callout). **Applies to ALL commit-producing operations**: bare `git commit`, `git commit --amend` (interactive or `-m`), `git rebase --continue` after edit/conflict resolution, `git cherry-pick` (incl. `--continue` after conflicts), `git am` / `git am --continue`, `git commit-tree` direct, `git merge --no-ff --commit` (when not fast-forward), and `git revert` (since revert produces a new commit). PR-comment-driven amendments are NOT routine - each amend is a NEW commit-producing event and requires its own commit-approval `ask_user` against the enumerated staged set. The rationale "the user already approved the original commit" does NOT carry forward to amends that change its content. **Why this matters**: the most common bypass pattern across multiple prior review cycles is the agent auto-staging (`git add -A`) and committing on the internal monologue "tests passed + ack done → ready to commit", skipping the user-staging review step entirely; the agent treats catalog ack as commit-license. **Required workflow**: (1) implementation completes (tests, lint, post-impl review); (2) the agent classifies with `git status --porcelain` - staged entries are the user's reviewed scope (commit as-is; do not re-diff/re-ask), and any unstaged / untracked change triggers a pause (`ask_user`: review-now / stage-for-me / skip-file / abort); the agent NEVER auto-stages code; (3) agent emits the literal `PRE-GIT SENTINEL` block (per `AGENTS.md` §0) declaring `user_diff_approval` as the reviewed-scope marker `staged-set:<tN>` (or `approved-via-pause:<tN>` for a paused file; a bare `pending` records unresolved unstaged changes and still BLOCKS) - a scope marker, NOT the sign-off; (4) the agent stages ONLY its own gate artifacts, then enumerates the full staged set; (5) the §0 `git commit` `ask_user` prints the enumerated staged set (user-code vs artifacts, flagged unexpected paths) - this is the human sign-off; emit `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` (per `pre-commit.md`); THEN `git commit` → (if `ask_user` re-confirms) `git push`. **High-signal failures**: the `git-add-dash-A` sub-case (agent ran `git add -A`, auto-staging code instead of letting the user stage as the review signal); the `amend-path` sub-case (agent treated `git commit --amend` + `git push --force-with-lease` after a PR-comment fix as routine and skipped the block; user pushback "we have done 2-3 amends related to this"); the `post-impl-review-skipped-after-pre-impl-panel-ready` sub-case (agent chained pre-impl-panel DESIGN_READY directly to commit without post-impl review or user approval); another case (agent finished a test phase, ran tests green, executed `git add` → `git commit` → `git push --force-with-lease` to a new branch without ever pausing for the user; user response: "never gave approval to git add or git commit, why are we doing this again, please fix the instructions"). Each occurrence reveals the same root cause: the agent does not pause at the implementation → git boundary because catalog ack feels like sufficient gate-passage. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: commit / push tool calls where a `PRE-GIT SENTINEL` block appears earlier in the same turn / recent conversation AND a `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` block records the commit-approval `ask_user` against the enumerated staged set; cases where the user proactively staged + approved the set without being asked; session-resume cases where the prior session committed but the new session is a no-op review only (no new commit); `git commit --amend --no-edit` that is mechanically restoring a previously-emitted committer timestamp without changing tree or message (e.g., immediately after a successful `git rebase --continue`) - the original commit's `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` block is preserved by reference (per `pre-commit.md` Skip conditions). **Audit method**: in any turn that calls `git add` of CODE paths (the agent may stage only gate artifacts) / `git commit` (any flag including `--amend`) / `git rebase --continue` / `git cherry-pick` (any flag including `--continue`) / `git am` (any flag) / `git commit-tree` / `git revert` / `git push` / `git push --force-with-lease` / `git push --force`, verify (a) the agent did NOT auto-stage code the user had not staged, and (b) a `PRE-GIT SENTINEL` block plus a `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` block recording the commit-approval against the enumerated staged set appear since the implementation phase began OR since the most recent commit-producing event. If either is absent, the §0 gate was bypassed. Process violation; track in `panel-misses.csv` under this slug. |  | HIGH |
| magic-string-numeric-literal | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW string or numeric literal added in the diff appearing in a non-test code path that controls program behavior (compared in a switch, passed as a config key, used as a route segment, used as a timeout, used as a buffer size, used as a retry count, used as a port number, used as a magic byte sentinel): verify the literal is either (a) declared as a named constant (`const`/`readonly static`) with intent-documenting name, (b) sourced from configuration with a documented default, or (c) genuinely so small/simple it would be more confusing as a constant (0, 1, -1, "", `string.Empty`). **High-signal failure**: `Task.Delay(3000)` - what does 3000 represent? Should be `Task.Delay(StartupSettleDelayMilliseconds)` or `Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(3))`. Similarly: `if (status == "InProgress")` should be `if (status == StatusValues.InProgress)`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: literals in tests (test data, expected values, parametrized cases); literals in resource-key constants files; mathematical literals (0, 1, -1, π, e, 100 for percent); error message format strings ("Failed to open {0}"); literals that ARE the value being defined (i.e., the constant declaration site itself). Use the diff to find NEW behavior-controlling literals; pre-existing magic values in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| razor-css-isolation-cross-file-class-reference | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.razor` markup file that references a CSS class name in its `class="..."` attributes: verify the class name is either (a) defined in the SAME component's `.razor.css` file (scope-isolated styles), or (b) defined in a global CSS file under `wwwroot/` / `_content/`, or (c) explicitly declared with the `::deep` combinator from a parent component's `.razor.css`. **High-signal failure**: `ManageItemsTab.razor` uses class names `db-entry-checkbox` and `db-entry-checkbox--visible`, but those styles are defined in `OrderRow.razor.css` (a SIBLING component's scope-isolated stylesheet). Blazor's CSS isolation appends a per-component `[b-XXX]` attribute selector to every rule in the sibling's `.razor.css` - the rules apply only to elements RENDERED by `OrderRow.razor`, not by `ManageItemsTab.razor`. Result: the markup compiles, the page renders, no warning emits, but the styles silently don't apply. **Fix patterns**: (a) move the shared rules to a global CSS file under `wwwroot/css/`; (b) duplicate the rules into BOTH components' `.razor.css` files (acceptable when the components have genuinely-separate concerns); (c) use the `::deep` combinator in a parent component's `.razor.css` if the parent renders both children (e.g., a `ModalChrome.razor.css` with `::deep .db-entry-checkbox { ... }` styling its descendants); (d) refactor the markup so the styled element lives in the owning component (move the checkbox markup INTO `OrderRow.razor`). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: class names defined in the same file's `.razor.css`; class names defined in a global stylesheet (`<link>` tag, `wwwroot/css/`); class names with the `::deep` combinator confirmed in a parent's `.razor.css`; framework-provided class names (Bootstrap's `btn`, Tailwind's `flex`, MudBlazor's `mud-button`). **Audit method**: for every NEW `class="<value>"` attribute in a modified `.razor` file, extract each class token. For each token, grep the SAME file's `.razor.css` first; if not found, grep `wwwroot/**/*.css` for the token; if not found, grep parent components' `.razor.css` for `::deep .<token>`; if still not found AND the token doesn't match a framework prefix, flag. |  | HIGH |
| aria-disabled-without-disabled-attribute | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified interactive element (`<button>`, `<a>`, `<input>`, `<select>`, `<textarea>`, custom Razor button component) in `.razor` / `.html` / `.cshtml` files that has `aria-disabled="true"` or `aria-disabled="@expr"` set: verify the element ALSO has the actual `disabled` HTML attribute (for native form controls) OR a click-handler guard that early-returns on the same condition. `aria-disabled` is an ACCESSIBILITY signal only - it tells screen readers "this is disabled" but does NOT prevent keyboard focus, click events, or form submission. Without the actual `disabled` attribute (or a guard), sighted keyboard / mouse users can still trigger the action. **High-signal failure**: `<button class="btn-primary" aria-disabled="@IsActionBlocked" @onclick="OnBulkActionClickAsync">Upgrade @SelectedCount</button>` - `aria-disabled` set but the button has no `disabled` attribute and `OnBulkActionClickAsync` doesn't early-return on `IsActionBlocked`. Sighted user clicks the visually-disabled button; the click handler runs anyway. **Fix patterns**: (a) add the actual `disabled` attribute alongside aria-disabled: `<button disabled="@IsActionBlocked" aria-disabled="@IsActionBlocked" ...>` (works for native `<button>` / `<input>`); (b) early-return in the click handler: `if (IsActionBlocked) return;` (acceptable but loses focus-disable semantic - keyboard user can still Tab onto it); (c) for custom Razor button components, plumb the disabled-ness through to the underlying `<button>` (don't render the `@onclick` at all when disabled). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: elements with both `aria-disabled` AND `disabled` set (correct pattern); elements where the click handler genuinely IS guarded with the same predicate (verify by reading the handler body - handler-side guard is weaker than `disabled` but defensible if focus-tab semantics are intentional); non-interactive elements with `aria-disabled` (e.g., a `<div role="button">` that's part of a custom widget where the role layer manages all interactions). **Audit method**: grep `aria-disabled\s*=` in NEW or modified `.razor` / `.html` / `.cshtml` files. For each match, verify the same element ALSO declares `disabled\s*=` OR the on-click handler's first statement early-returns on the same expression. If neither, flag. |  | HIGH |
| readonly-interface-leaks-backing-mutable-collection | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified public/internal API surface in the diff that exposes a property, return value, event-args field, or test-fake-stored value typed as a read-only collection interface (`IReadOnlyList<T>`, `IReadOnlyCollection<T>`, `IReadOnlyDictionary<K,V>`, `IReadOnlySet<T>`, `IEnumerable<T>` used as a "read-only" contract): verify the BACKING storage cannot be downcast by a consumer to mutate it. **Hard truth - what IS and is NOT immutable**: a consumer holding `IReadOnlyList<T>` CAN cast to `T[]` (and mutate elements) when the backing is an array, AND CAN cast to `List<T>` (and call `.Add` / `.RemoveAt`) when the backing is a `List<T>`. The ONLY truly downcast-safe options are: `ImmutableArray<T>` / `ImmutableList<T>` / `FrozenSet<T>` / `ImmutableDictionary<K,V>` (immutable storage; casts to mutable types fail at runtime), or `ReadOnlyCollection<T>` via `.AsReadOnly()` (a wrapper that throws `NotSupportedException` on mutation). Three known leak patterns: (a) **direct backing-field return** - `public IReadOnlyList<T> Items => _items;` where `_items` is a `List<T>` field. A caller does `((List<T>)x.Items).Add(...)` and mutates the producer's state. Fix: return `_items.AsReadOnly()` (wraps to throw on cast-mutate) OR change the backing to `ImmutableArray<T>` AND expose it typed as `ImmutableArray<T>` (not `IReadOnlyList<T>` - exposing immutable storage through a read-only interface signals "callers may try to downcast"; expose the immutable type directly). (b) **event args holding caller-supplied collection** - `public sealed record StartedEventArgs(IReadOnlyList<string> FileNames);` populated via `new StartedEventArgs(callerList)` (no copy at all - caller mutates the list, subscribers see mutation) OR `new StartedEventArgs(callerList.ToList())` (defensive copy of references, but the COPY is a `List<string>` that subscribers can still downcast). Fix: `new StartedEventArgs(ImmutableArray.CreateRange(callerList))` AND change the record's field type to `ImmutableArray<string>` so the contract is truly immutable. (c) **test fake stores caller's collection** - `public List<IReadOnlyList<string>> BatchCalls { get; } = new(); public Task X(IReadOnlyList<string> names) { BatchCalls.Add(names); ... }` - stores the CALLER's instance. If test code later mutates the original list (`fileNames.Add("extra")`), the recorded call-history retroactively changes. Fix: `BatchCalls.Add(ImmutableArray.CreateRange(names))` (or `[..names]` if the storage's static type is `ImmutableArray<string>` so the compiler picks the immutable conversion). **High-signal failures**: (a) `BatchStartedEventArgs.FileNames` populated via `.ToList()` - copy of references but still downcast-mutable, fix is `ImmutableArray<string>`; (b) `FakeOrderService.BatchCalls.Add(fileNames)` stores caller's list; (c) `collection-cast-before-defensive-copy` (originally a separate slug, now folded here). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: returns / fields typed as `ImmutableArray<T>` / `ImmutableList<T>` / `FrozenSet<T>` / `FrozenDictionary<K,V>` / `ImmutableSortedSet<T>` directly (not through a read-only interface); returns of `ReadOnlyCollection<T>` from `.AsReadOnly()` calls; private fields returned ONLY by methods within the same class (no consumer to leak to - verify by reading the access modifier AND that no public method returns the field). **Acceptable but suboptimal**: arrays typed as `IReadOnlyList<T>` AND a maintainer comment documenting "callers MUST NOT cast" - code-comment is weaker than type-enforced immutability but defensible when the consumer surface is small and audited. **Audit method**: for every NEW public/internal property / event-args field / test-fake-stored value typed as `IReadOnly*`, view the producer site. Verify the produced value is `ImmutableArray<T>`-typed (or another immutable collection), OR is wrapped in `.AsReadOnly()`, OR is stored via `ImmutableArray.CreateRange(...)`. If the producer stores a `T[]` or `List<T>` and exposes via `IReadOnly*`, the cast-and-mutate leak exists - flag. |  | HIGH |
| interface-event-non-nullable-vs-impl-nullable-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified event declaration in the diff on an `interface` / `abstract class` whose nullable-reference-type annotation does NOT match the concrete implementation's annotation: verify both sides agree. C# nullable-reference-types (`#nullable enable`) treat `event Action StateChanged;` (non-nullable) and `event Action? StateChanged;` (nullable) as DIFFERENT signatures from a nullable-flow perspective. In a project with `<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>` AND `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>`, an interface declaring `event Action StateChanged;` while the implementing class declares `event Action? StateChanged;` produces CS8767-family nullable-mismatch warnings → build fails. **Precondition**: this rule fires ONLY when the project uses `Nullable=enable` AND `TreatWarningsAsErrors=true` (the default for most modern .NET 8+ libraries). Projects with `Nullable=disable` or WaaE off may not surface the diagnostic until later. **High-signal failure**: `IAnnouncementService.StateChanged` declared as non-nullable `event Action StateChanged;`. Implementation `AnnouncementService` uses `event Action? StateChanged;` (the de facto convention since events have no subscribers by default). Mismatch produced WaaE build failure on PR queue. **Fix patterns**: (a) declare both nullable (`event Action? StateChanged;` on interface AND implementation) - this is the dominant convention because events START null until first subscriber adds; (b) declare both non-nullable AND ensure the implementation initializes with a no-op handler (`event Action StateChanged = static () => { };`); (c) use `EventHandler` / `EventHandler<T>` delegate types - those are nullable by convention. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: event declarations in projects with `Nullable=disable` (no warning will fire); event declarations whose interface AND implementation already agree. **Audit method**: for every NEW `event` declaration in an `interface` or `abstract class` modifier in the diff, grep the codebase for implementing classes (`grep -r "class \w*\s*:\s*IXxx"` then look for `event` declarations of the same name); verify the nullable annotation matches. If interface is non-nullable AND implementation is nullable (or vice versa), flag. |  | HIGH |
| razor-empty-css-selector-foiled-by-whitespace-text-nodes | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `.razor.css` / `.css` file in the diff that uses the `:empty` pseudo-class selector (e.g., `.toolbar-leading:empty { display: none; }` or `.filter-error:empty { display: none; }`): verify the corresponding markup in the `.razor` file does NOT render any whitespace text node inside the targeted element. `:empty` matches ONLY elements with NO children - and a single newline / indent between tags COUNTS as a whitespace text node in the DOM, defeating `:empty`. **High-signal failure**: `DatabaseToolsLogView.razor.css:9` has `.toolbar-leading:empty { display: none; }` but the Razor markup renders `<div class="toolbar-leading">\n    @ToolbarLeadingContent\n</div>` - the newline + indent between the opening tag and the `@ToolbarLeadingContent` directive becomes a text node even when the RenderFragment is null. The selector never matches and the empty slot remains visible. Second instance: `DatabaseToolsModal.razor.css` `.filter-error:empty { display: none; }` with markup `<span class="filter-error">\n    @ErrorMessage\n</span>`. **Fix patterns**: (a) render the directive on the SAME line as the element tags: `<div class="toolbar-leading">@ToolbarLeadingContent</div>` (no whitespace between tags); (b) use a conditional in the Razor to omit the element when empty: `@if (ToolbarLeadingContent is not null) { <div class="toolbar-leading">@ToolbarLeadingContent</div> }`; (c) switch CSS to use `display: none` by default and `display: block` only when the slot has a marker class added by the Razor when content is present (`@if (HasContent) { <div class="toolbar-leading has-content"> ... }`); (d) avoid `:empty` entirely - use an `@if`-controlled CSS class. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `:empty` selectors on elements whose markup is GUARANTEED to be tag-only (e.g., `<input>` self-closing tags, or markup `<div></div>` with no whitespace); `:empty` selectors used as a fallback paired with explicit JS / Razor logic that strips whitespace. **Audit method**: regex-grep `:empty\s*\{` in NEW or modified `.razor.css` / `.css` files. For each match, identify the targeted class/selector and grep the corresponding `.razor` for the markup using that class. If the markup has ANY whitespace (newline, indent, space) between the element's opening tag and its content directive, flag. |  | HIGH |
| razor-button-missing-type-defaults-to-submit | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `<button>` element rendered inside a Blazor `<EditForm>` or a raw HTML `<form>` in a `.razor` file - OR any `<button>` that newly falls inside a NEW or modified `<EditForm>`/`<form>` (e.g. a refactor that wraps existing buttons in a form) - verify the button's opening tag has an explicit, statically-determinable `type` whose value is one of `button`, `submit`, or `reset`. `<button>`'s `type` is an HTML ENUMERATED attribute whose missing-value default AND invalid-value default are BOTH the Submit state: a `<button>` with no `type`, an empty `type=`, or a typo (`type=buton`, `type=onclick`) ALL resolve to `type=submit`. A submit-resolving button click submits the enclosing form IN ADDITION to running any `@onclick` handler. Two failure modes by container: (1) inside a Blazor `<EditForm>`, the submit runs the full `EditContext` validation pass plus `OnValidSubmit`/`OnSubmit`/`OnInvalidSubmit` - the click runs `@onclick` AND fires the submit path; (2) inside a raw `<form>` - EVEN WITH NO `@onsubmit` handler - the browser performs a NATIVE submit (full-page POST/reload to the current URL), tearing down Blazor SPA / circuit state (often the most damaging variant - do NOT treat a handler-less `<form>` as safe). Double-fire sub-case: a button with an EXPLICIT `type=submit` (or a framework component that resolves to Submit) that ALSO carries an `@onclick` runs BOTH the `@onclick` and the form submit; submission should be owned by `EditForm` `OnValidSubmit` / the form `@onsubmit`, not a competing button `@onclick`. High-signal failures: a secondary button like `<button @onclick=Cancel>Cancel</button>` or an icon `<button @onclick=RemoveItem>` inside a form (no `type` -> submits); a typo `<button type=buton @onclick=Cancel>` (invalid -> still submits); a `<button type=submit @onclick=Save>` where `OnValidSubmit` also saves (double-fire). Symptoms: spurious validation on an unrelated click, a Save/persist running when the user clicked Cancel or a row-delete, double execution, a full-page reload that drops SPA state, unexpected navigation. Fix patterns: (a) add an explicit `type=button` to EVERY non-submit button inside the form; (b) give the intended submit exactly one explicit `type=submit`; (c) own submission via `EditForm` `OnValidSubmit` / the form `@onsubmit` and REMOVE the competing button `@onclick`; (d) if a click genuinely must not submit, add `@onclick:preventDefault`. Acceptable (do NOT flag): buttons NOT inside any `<EditForm>`/`<form>` range; buttons with an explicit `type=button` or `type=reset`; an explicit `type=submit` button whose only `@onclick` (if any) is an ORTHOGONAL side effect meant to run alongside submission (e.g. setting a submitting-spinner flag, telemetry, closing a menu) - flag it ONLY when the `@onclick` DUPLICATES/competes with the submit (e.g. `@onclick=Save` while `OnValidSubmit` also persists); a no-`type` button carrying BUTTON-LEVEL `@onclick:preventDefault` - this cancels the click's default action (the submit) so the form is not submitted (CAVEATS: a CONDITIONAL `preventDefault=@flag` still submits when the flag is false; `@onclick:stopPropagation` does NOT prevent submit). NOTE form-level `@onsubmit:preventDefault` is NOT equivalent and is NOT a safe carve-out: it cancels only the native page-reload while the form's `@onsubmit`/`OnValidSubmit` handler STILL fires, so a no-`type` button still spuriously triggers that handler (safe only if the form has no submit handler or its firing is intended). A button whose `type` is supplied dynamically (`type=@(expr)` or via an `@attributes` splat) - the literal token is absent but may be set at runtime - confirm before flagging (manual review, NOT auto-pass). Blazor static-SSR / `[SupplyParameterFromForm]` POST forms, where an in-form submit button IS the intended mechanism. Non-`<button>` clickables (`<a>`, `<div role=button>`, `<span @onclick>`), which never default to submit. Framework button COMPONENTS (`<MudButton>`, `<FluentButton>`, `<RadzenButton>`, Blazorise `<Button>`, etc.) - resolve the component's ACTUAL default `ButtonType`/`Type`/`HtmlType` for that library AND version plus any explicit override, and exempt ONLY when it resolves to Button; do NOT assume by component family (defaults vary across libraries/versions and a component may render an underlying native submit button); an UNKNOWN default is a judgment call, NOT an auto-pass. Limitations (honest): this is a review-pass-only HEURISTIC the reviewer adjudicates, not a mechanical rule - a single-line regex cannot reliably range-track multi-line form spans; cross-component containment (a `<button>` rendered by a CHILD component that lands inside a parent's form) is not statically detectable per file - note it, do not claim coverage. Audit method: in each NEW or modified `.razor` file, scan case-insensitively for `<EditForm ...>`/`</EditForm>` and `<form ...>`/`</form>` boundaries (opening tags may carry attributes; ranges span many lines) and pair them with a PER-KIND stack/counter (EditForm with EditForm, form with form) so nested or sequential forms are bounded correctly. Within each range, parse each `<button` OPENING tag from `<button` through its OWN closing `>` (support multi-line opening tags), then read the button's own `type` - match `type` as a standalone attribute token (preceded by whitespace or `<button`, NOT by `-` or other name characters, and NOT inside a quoted value, so `data-type=`/`formtype=`/`title="...type=..."` do NOT count), case-insensitive. FLAG when the button has no own `type` token OR its literal value (trimmed, lowercased) is not one of `button`/`submit`/`reset`; do NOT flag a static `type=button`/`type=reset`/`type=submit`; for a dynamic `type=@(...)` or an `@attributes` splat, mark manual-review (NOT auto-pass). SEPARATELY, flag any button with an EXPLICIT `type=submit` (or a submit-resolving framework component) whose opening tag ALSO carries an `@onclick`, for the reviewer to adjudicate competing-vs-orthogonal (the double-fire sub-case). Candidates from this scan are then adjudicated against the Acceptable list above (e.g. a button-level `@onclick:preventDefault` clears the primary flag). Buttons outside all form ranges are out of scope. |  | HIGH |
| regex-instance-without-matchtimeout-allows-catastrophic-backtracking | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `new Regex(...)` constructor call in the diff, OR for every method that accepts a `Regex` parameter from a caller, verify the regex was constructed with a finite `MatchTimeout` (e.g., `new Regex(pattern, RegexOptions.None, TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1))`). A `Regex` constructed without a timeout has `MatchTimeout = Regex.InfiniteMatchTimeout` - pathological inputs against patterns with nested quantifiers (e.g., `(a+)+$`) cause catastrophic backtracking that hangs the thread indefinitely (CPU pegged, no exception, no timeout). **High-signal failure**: `ShowProvidersOperation` and `CreateDatabaseOperation` both accept a `Regex` parameter from the request DTO; the operations wrap pattern execution in `try { match.Success } catch (RegexMatchTimeoutException) { ... }` - but `RegexMatchTimeoutException` ONLY fires when the Regex was constructed with a finite `MatchTimeout`. Callers can pass a `Regex` with infinite timeout and the catch never fires. **Fix patterns**: (a) construct regex with timeout at the site: `new Regex(pattern, RegexOptions.None, TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1))`; (b) for methods accepting `Regex` from callers, REJECT regex instances with `regex.MatchTimeout == Regex.InfiniteMatchTimeout` at the entry point (`ArgumentException.ThrowIf(regex.MatchTimeout == Regex.InfiniteMatchTimeout, nameof(regex))`); (c) better, accept the pattern STRING and construct internally with a known-safe timeout; (d) use `[GeneratedRegex(pattern, RegexOptions.None, matchTimeoutMilliseconds: 1000)]` source generator (compile-time guaranteed timeout). **Note**: this rule is `review-pass-only` (not rg-scoped) because `new Regex(...)` calls commonly span multiple lines (multi-line argument formatting), which line-based rg cannot reliably parse. A reviewer must read the full constructor argument list to determine whether the third (MatchTimeout) arg is present. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `Regex` constructed with the `[GeneratedRegex(..., matchTimeoutMilliseconds: ...)]` source-generator attribute; `Regex` instances stored in static field with an explicit timeout (`private static readonly Regex Pattern = new(..., TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(500))`); test code constructing regex against known-safe patterns; consumed-internally regex where the pattern + input are both compile-time constants. **Audit method**: review every NEW `new Regex(` site in the diff (multi-line aware) - check that the constructor includes a `TimeSpan` argument. Review every NEW method signature accepting `Regex` and verify the entry point rejects infinite-timeout instances. |  | HIGH |
| namespace-mismatch-symbol-referenced-without-using | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW symbol reference in the diff (a type name, an extension method call, an `@using` directive in Razor) where the file's namespace cannot reach the symbol's declaring namespace through C# simple-name lookup OR explicit `using` directive OR `_Imports.razor`: verify the symbol's namespace is reachable. C# `dotnet build` catches most cases via CS0246 / CS0103 - this rule still applies for THREE surfaces where the failure is NOT compile-time-fast: (a) **Razor `@using` omissions in `.razor` files** where the type is only referenced in markup - the error surfaces at Razor compile time (slower CI roundtrip) or runtime; (b) **doc-comment `<see cref="..."/>` references** where invalid cref produces a warning the IDE doesn't always surface immediately AND that becomes an error under WaaE; (c) **conditional-compilation guards** where the `using` is inside `#if DEBUG` but the type usage is unconditional - the missing-using surfaces only in Release builds. **C# lookup is hierarchical (per ECMA-334 §13.8)**: simple-name lookup walks the enclosing namespaces from innermost to outermost, so a type declared in a PARENT namespace IS visible from any DESCENDANT namespace WITHOUT a `using` directive. The opposite is NOT true - a parent namespace cannot reach a CHILD namespace's symbols without an explicit `using`. See `FP-1: parent-namespace-not-in-scope` for the canonical false-positive pattern this rule MUST avoid. **High-signal failure (Razor sub-case)**: `Foo.Bar.Views.Page.razor` markup references `<MyComponent />` where `MyComponent` lives in a SIBLING namespace `Foo.Bar.Components` - no `@using Foo.Bar.Components` in `_Imports.razor` or in the file. The C# compiler catches `using`-omissions in `.razor.cs`, but markup-only references go through the Razor compile pipeline, where the error message is less direct and may not surface until first render in dev workflows. **High-signal failure (cref sub-case)**: `<see cref="ICriticalAbstraction.Method"/>` doc-comment in a file whose namespace doesn't reach `ICriticalAbstraction` - under `<GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>` + WaaE the missing-symbol becomes a build error that's only emitted when docs are produced (Release / publish builds). **Fix patterns**: (a) add the explicit `using` at the top of the file (for `.cs`); (b) add `@using Sibling.Namespace` to `_Imports.razor` (for Razor); (c) fully-qualify the reference (`global::Foo.Bar.Components.MyComponent`) when an explicit `using` would conflict; (d) move the symbol to a `GlobalUsings.cs` if used pervasively. **Acceptable (do NOT flag - these are valid C# OR caught by FP-1)**: symbols in a PARENT namespace of the file's namespace (per ECMA-334 §13.8 - see FP-1); symbols imported via explicit `using`; symbols imported via project-level `<Using Include="..."/>` MSBuild items or `GlobalUsings.cs`; framework types (`System.*`, `Microsoft.*`) where `<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>` is on; cases where the C# build would catch a real CS0246/CS0103 (rule fires only for the 3 surfaces above where the build doesn't catch fast). **Audit method**: for every NEW Razor markup tag, `@using`, or `<see cref>` reference in the diff, identify the containing namespace of the symbol via grep / IDE lookup. If the symbol is in a SIBLING / unrelated / child namespace (NOT a parent namespace) AND no matching `using` / `_Imports.razor` entry exists, flag. Cross-check against FP-1 before flagging - if the supposed-missing namespace is a parent of the file's namespace, the rule does NOT apply. | fp-1 | HIGH |
| facade-discards-inner-result-discriminator | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified wrapper / facade / adapter method in the diff that wraps a result-discriminator-returning inner call (`Result<T>`, custom `Outcome` enum, `(bool Success, T Value)` tuple, `XxxResult` record with a `WasSomethingHappened` flag): verify the wrapper PROPAGATES the discriminator distinction up the call chain. Wrappers that collapse a multi-valued result to a single bool (`return true` on no-exception, `return false` on exception) silently lose meaningful caller-distinctions. **High-signal failure**: `MenuActionService.ShowModalAsync(...)` calls `await ModalCoordinator.TryOpenAsync(...)` which returns `ModalOpenResult` with a `WasOpened` boolean (false on preemption-veto). The wrapper does `await ModalCoordinator.TryOpenAsync(...); return true;` - ignoring the `WasOpened` field entirely. Callers (e.g., `NotificationBanner.OpenSettingsAsync`) expect `false` when Settings failed to open (veto case) so they can show an error. With `return true` always, the error path silently never fires. **Fix patterns**: (a) propagate the discriminator: `var result = await ModalCoordinator.TryOpenAsync(...); return result.WasOpened;`; (b) return the inner result type unchanged from the wrapper (`Task<ModalOpenResult>` not `Task<bool>`); (c) if the wrapper's `bool` return has DIFFERENT semantics from the inner discriminator, document the mapping explicitly in XML doc AND verify the mapping is correct for every inner discriminator value. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: wrappers that genuinely don't care about the inner discriminator (e.g., a fire-and-forget kick-off where caller never branches on the outcome - verify by reading caller code); wrappers where the inner result has only ONE distinguishable outcome (no discriminator to lose); wrappers whose `bool` return is documented to mean "called successfully without exception" rather than "operation achieved its goal". **Audit method**: for every NEW wrapper-style method in the diff (signature pattern: takes args, calls one or two inner methods, returns `bool` or `Task<bool>`), view the inner method's return type. If the inner return type has named fields / properties / discriminator values BEYOND success/failure, the wrapper's collapse to `bool` likely loses information. Cross-check by viewing the wrapper's CALLER sites for branches on the bool return - if a caller's `if (await wrapper.X()) { /* A */ } else { /* B */ }` would benefit from a third outcome, the wrapper is too lossy. |  | HIGH |
| record-non-nullable-ctor-validation | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `record` / `record struct` / `class` with a primary constructor declaration in the diff that has one or more non-nullable reference-type parameters (`string`, `IReadOnlyList<T>`, custom class): verify the constructor body OR a `[Required]`-equivalent attribute validates that the caller did not pass `null` via the `null!` escape hatch or `default(T)` cast. C# nullable annotations are compile-time hints - at runtime, callers can still pass `null` via `null!`, reflection, deserialization, or `default(T)`. Later, `session.Permissions.Count` throws NRE in some unrelated method. **Fix patterns**: (a) explicit null check in primary constructor body: `public sealed record UserSession(string Id, IReadOnlyList<Permission> Permissions) { public string Id { get; } = Id ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(Id)); public IReadOnlyList<Permission> Permissions { get; } = Permissions ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(Permissions)); }`; (b) use `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(Id); ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(Permissions);` in the primary constructor body (records support this); (c) use System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis `[NotNull]` attribute pairing where applicable. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: records with all-nullable reference params; records explicitly documented as "framework-deserialization only, post-construction validation occurs in the deserializer wrapper"; primitive struct params (value types can't be null); records whose construction sites are 100% within the same assembly AND audited to never pass `null!`. **Audit method**: for every NEW `record` / primary-constructor `class` declaration in the diff, list each parameter's nullability. For each non-nullable reference-type parameter, verify the constructor body or property initializer includes a null check. If absent, flag. |  | HIGH |
| state-read-stale-after-event-publication | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW pattern in the diff where (a) one method PUBLISHES an event / raises a notification / completes a TCS / dispatches to a Fluxor action that has a corresponding event handler / subscriber doing reactive state-clear / pull-from-store work, AND (b) ANOTHER method or call site READS the same state via a getter / property / `Get*` method between the publish-event and the subscriber's reactive-clear completing: verify the reader's contract allows observing the PRE-clear state, OR the reader is forced to wait for the subscriber to converge. State-reads in the window between event-publish and reactive-clear return STALE state - and the reader may not realize it's racing. **High-signal failure**: `ServiceRegistry.UnregisterAsync(id)` raises `RegistrationRemoved` event and returns. A Fluxor effect subscribes to that event and does `state.Registrations[id] = null` reactively - but `ServiceRegistry.GetRegistration(id)` (a synchronous lookup) still returns the old registration for the duration of the effect's dispatch. Callers calling `Get` right after `Unregister` see stale data. **Fix patterns**: (a) make the publisher do the state-clear inline (synchronous) BEFORE raising the event - readers immediately see clean state; (b) make the reader async + wait for the subscriber's completion via TCS; (c) document the eventual-consistency window explicitly in XML doc on the reader AND ensure no caller depends on immediate consistency; (d) include the post-clear state in the event args so consumers don't need to re-read. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: read paths explicitly documented as eventually-consistent; reads on a different observable (the cleared state is on store A; the read is from store B that's not yet affected); patterns where the publish-then-read interleaving is forbidden by an outer lock. **Audit method**: for every NEW event-raise / TCS-complete / Fluxor `Dispatch(action)` in the diff, identify the subscriber(s). If a subscriber mutates state observable via a synchronous getter elsewhere in the codebase, flag the read sites that could race. |  | HIGH |
| least-privilege-audit-required-on-visibility-delta | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** For every commit whose diff contains a visibility / export / mutability surface delta - adds a public/exported type or member, widens visibility, removes `sealed`/`final`, adds or widens a constructor / setter, exposes a field, changes package / module exports, adds an exported Go top-level identifier, adds a Rust `pub` declaration (`pub fn`, `pub struct`, `pub enum`, `pub trait`, `pub mod`, bare `pub` widening from `pub(crate)`), etc. - verify the POST-CODE-CHANGE LEDGER block in the agent's text output earlier in the conversation contains a `touched-file-LPA` field with value `ran (N findings, K unjustified)` OR `N/A - <reason>` where the reason cites a specific carve-out from `least-privilege-audit.md` (framework-mandated visibility per least-privilege-audit.md:40, public SDK / NuGet / npm consumer surface per G6 conservative default, test-only widening with `[InternalsVisibleTo]` already in place, etc.). **Why this matters**: the touched-file LPA is the cheapest moment to prevent new visibility leaks; the playbook is invoked by `post-code-change.md` step 2 BUT only triggers when the agent recognizes the delta. production-source change is purely deleting code (no new behavior to test). **Audit method**: in any commit where `git diff <base>..HEAD --name-only` shows files matching `**/*Tests.*` / `**/*Test.*` / `**/*.spec.*` / `**/__tests__/**` OR `git diff <base>..HEAD` against production source files shows added lines containing `if `/`else `/`switch `/`case `/`when `/`?:`/`try `/`catch `/`throw `/`return ` (new control flow or termination) OR added lines starting with method-declaration patterns OR new state-mutating assignments to instance fields, locate the POST-CODE-CHANGE LEDGER block.
