| implementation-planning-required-on-nontrivial-final-diff | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** POST-IMPL companion to rule 2. For every commit whose `git diff <base>..HEAD` (final state) is non-trivial per the closed-enumeration triviality criterion in `pre-implementation.md` G6 (NOT in `{single-line typo, single-property/single-config-key tweak, comment-only edit, formatting-only edit}` AND has any other change in diff): verify the LEDGER `gates.pre-impl-playbook-decisions.implementation-planning` is `invoked` OR `required-but-skipped: "<re-confirmation>"`. Fires on `missing` / `not-applicable` / `offered-and-declined` (INVALID for REQUIRED-class) AND on `not-required-trigger-not-detected` (final-diff non-trivial contradicts the pre-impl "trivial" sentinel - re-entry must update per `pre-implementation.md` G6 re-entry clause). **Why this matters**: catches the scope-grew-during-implementation bypass where G6 originally emitted `not-required-trigger-not-detected` (trivial at pre-impl time) but the final diff is non-trivial. Without rule 13, an agent could legitimately classify the change as trivial at pre-impl time, then silently grow the scope during implementation without re-entering G6, and ship without implementation-planning. **Satisfiability path**: when scope grows mid-implementation, the agent re-enters G6 per `pre-implementation.md` re-entry clause and UPDATES the LEDGER decision line; once updated to `invoked` or `required-but-skipped`, rule 13 does not fire. **High-signal failures**: final diff is non-trivial, ledger says `implementation-planning: not-required-trigger-not-detected` (missed re-entry); final diff is non-trivial, ledger missing the field. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: final diff matches the closed-enumeration triviality criterion (rule does not apply); final diff non-trivial AND ledger decision = `invoked` (playbook ran); final diff non-trivial AND ledger decision = `required-but-skipped: "<re-confirmation>"` (User-skip policy applied). **Audit method**: in any commit where `git diff <base>..HEAD` does NOT match one of the 4 closed-enumeration trivial cases (single-line typo OR single-property/single-config-key tweak OR comment-only edit OR formatting-only edit) AND has any other change in diff, locate the LEDGER block. If `gates.pre-impl-playbook-decisions.implementation-planning` is anything other than `invoked` or `required-but-skipped`, fire. Track in `panel-misses.csv`. |  | HIGH |
| nsubstitute-extraneous-arg-any-call-pollutes-received-history | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW NSubstitute helper / Arrange block in the diff containing a standalone `_substitute.Method(Arg.Any<T>(), ...)` invocation OUTSIDE a `When(...)` / `WhenForAnyArgs(...)` / `Returns(...)` / `ReturnsForAnyArgs(...)` / `Do(...)` configuration context: verify the call is intentional. NSubstitute records the standalone call as a real invocation in the substitute's call history - silently inflating any future `Received(N).Method(...)` assertion by 1 and breaking the helper's intent-clarity. **High-signal failure**: helper `CapturedMoreMenuItemsAsync` started with `_menuService.OpenAt(Arg.Any<double>(), Arg.Any<double>(), Arg.Any<IReadOnlyList<MenuItem>>(), Arg.Any<bool>(), Arg.Any<bool>());` before the `WhenForAnyArgs(...).Do(...)` setup. Any test using the helper would see `Received(2)` instead of `Received(1)` on call-count assertions. xUnit re-instantiates the test class per method so cross-test contamination is bounded, but the noise still breaks per-test assertions. **Fix patterns**: (a) DELETE the standalone call - `WhenForAnyArgs(s => s.Method(default, default, ...))` already specifies the method signature for the matcher; (b) move the matcher INTO a `Returns` chain: `_substitute.Method(Arg.Any<...>()).Returns(value)`; (c) use `When(s => s.Method(...)).Do(callback)` for capture patterns. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: `Arg.Any` inside `Returns(...)` / `When(...)` / `Do(...)` blocks (NSubstitute recognizes these contexts; no real invocation recorded); standalone calls in the Act section that ARE the test's subject. **Audit method**: regex-grep `_\w+\.\w+\([^)]*Arg\.Any` in NEW or modified `*Tests.cs` files. For each match, view the enclosing statement and verify it's inside `When*(...)` / `Returns*(...)` / `Do(...)`. If standalone, flag. |  | LOW |
| js-interop-attach-without-symmetric-detach | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `_jsModule.InvokeVoidAsync("attach"\|"init"\|"register"\|"subscribe", ...)` call in the diff (or equivalent JS-side resource-registration interop): verify a SYMMETRIC `detach`/`teardown`/`unregister`/`unsubscribe` call exists in EVERY corresponding lifecycle path that could orphan the registration (DisposeAsync, state-transitions that re-render the underlying element, conditional re-attach paths). Asymmetric attach/detach contracts leave JS-side resources (event listeners, observers, intervals, WeakMap entries) bound to stale elements. Modern browsers' GC reclaims orphaned DOM-element-keyed entries via `WeakMap`, so the leak is often self-cleaning - but the asymmetry is still a real correctness/clarity issue, and defense-in-depth matters when the JS shim doesn't use `WeakMap`. **High-signal failure**: `OnAfterRenderAsync` gated `attach` on `_tablistRef` being rendered AND reset `_tabKeyShimAttached = false` when transitioning back to loading/error state - but never called `detach` at that transition. WeakMap-shim self-cleans via GC, but contract was asymmetric. **Fix patterns**: (a) add an explicit `await _jsModule.InvokeVoidAsync("detach", _ref)` in the C# state-transition path BEFORE flipping the C#-side "attached" flag back to false, with same exception-swallowing as the attach branch (`JSDisconnectedException`, `JSException`); (b) hoist attach/detach pairing into a single `try/finally` if lifecycle is a single render; (c) document the WeakMap-cleanup-only invariant inline IF intentionally relying on JS GC. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: attach/detach pairs already symmetric in same lifecycle path; one-shot JS calls that don't register persistent resources (e.g., `prompt`, `getBoundingRect`); calls within `DisposeAsync` where the component is being torn down anyway. **Audit method**: for every NEW `InvokeVoidAsync\(.*("attach"\|"init"\|"register"\|"subscribe")\)` call site in `.cs`/`.razor.cs`, grep the same file for the symmetric `("detach"\|"teardown"\|"unregister"\|"unsubscribe")` call. If only one half exists outside `DisposeAsync`, flag. |  | LOW |
| multi-rung-focus-cascade-preemptive-flag-clear | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified focus-restoration / attention-routing cascade in the diff with multiple fallback rungs (target → secondary → terminal-fallback) where each rung is gated on a flag (`_pendingFocusX`, `_pendingFocusY`, `_pendingFallback`): verify that PREEMPTIVELY CLEARING a downstream-fallback flag BEFORE attempting the current rung does NOT silently end the cascade when the current rung fails. The pattern `flag = false; if (TryGetX(out var x)) await SafelyAsync(x);` clears the downstream signal even when `TryGetX` returns false OR `SafelyAsync` swallows an exception - no fallback fires in the same render pass. **High-signal failure**: `OnAfterRenderAsync` cascade for `_pendingFocusTab` branch set `_pendingFocusToActiveTab = false` BEFORE `await ElementFocus.SafelyAsync(tabRef)`. If `_tabButtonRefs.TryGetValue` returned false OR `SafelyAsync` swallowed an exception, no active-tab fallback fired in the same render. **Fix patterns**: (a) use a `Try*Async` variant that returns `bool` success indicator (`bool focused = await ElementFocus.TrySafelyAsync(target)`); set the fallback flag to `!focused` so failure chains to the next rung; (b) restructure cascade as a sequence of `if (!focused && ...)` branches instead of `if / else if` so each rung can fall through to the next on failure; (c) defer flag-clear until AFTER the rung's focus call succeeds. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: terminal-fallback rungs (no further fallback possible); cascades where each rung's failure is genuinely terminal; single-rung cascades. **Audit method**: in NEW focus cascades, look for `_pending\w*\s*=\s*false` statements IMMEDIATELY before `await.*FocusAsync` / `SafelyAsync` calls. Verify the cascade can recover if the focus call fails silently. If not, flag. |  | LOW |
| positional-record-property-override-without-init-breaks-with | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW positional `record` (or `record struct`) declaration in the diff with a property override that shadows a positional parameter - e.g., `public sealed record Foo(T Bar) { public T Bar { get; } = Bar ?? throw ...; }` - verify the override declares `init;` setter, not just `get;`. Without `init`, the override SHADOWS the auto-generated positional property's `init` setter, making `foo with { Bar = ... }` fail to compile. **High-signal failure**: `AddToPresetIntent` added a `Filter` property override `public SavedFilter Filter { get; } = Filter ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(Filter));` to add runtime null validation per the `record-non-nullable-ctor-validation` slug fix. The `{ get; }` (no `init`) shadowed the auto-generated init setter - any future `intent with { Filter = otherFilter }` would fail at compile time. **Fix patterns**: (a) minimal - add `init;`: `public SavedFilter Filter { get; init; } = Filter ?? throw ...;` (restores `with` semantics; validation still fires on construction; `with { Filter = null! }` would bypass re-validation per standard C# trade-off); (b) explicit-ctor - replace positional syntax with explicit constructor that validates + manual init properties; (c) use a backing field with validation in the `init` setter (`init => _filter = value ?? throw ...`) - fires on `with` mutations too but is verbose. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: property overrides that intentionally drop `with`-mutation support (cite the design rationale inline); records explicitly documented as "immutable, never mutated via `with`"; classes (non-record) where `with` doesn't apply. **Symmetric concern**: this slug is the inverse of `record-non-nullable-ctor-validation` - applying THAT slug's fix without preserving `init` introduces THIS bug. Future record-validation work MUST consider both. **Audit method**: regex-grep `public\s+(sealed\s+)?(partial\s+)?record(\s+struct)?\s+\w+\s*\(` in NEW source files, then within each match find `public\s+\w+\s+\w+\s*\{\s*get;\s*\}` declarations inside the record body that override a parameter name. If `{ get; }` without `init`, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| test-comment-claims-mechanism-test-doesnt-use | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or substantively rewritten test method in the diff with multi-line `//` comments describing how the test verifies something: verify the named verification mechanism MATCHES what the test body actually does. Comments referencing "reflection-based count", "subscriber count introspection", "via NSubstitute call history", "via mock verification" - when the test body actually uses a simpler oracle (no-exception, return-value check, side-effect observation, render-no-error) - create false impressions of test complexity AND inflate cognitive load for maintainers. **Sub-case of `code-comment-mismatch`** specific to test code patterns. **High-signal failure**: `DisposeAsync_UnsubscribesFromMenuServiceStateChanged` test had 7 lines of comments claiming "Verify via reflection-based count" + "Substitute doesn't expose subscriber count directly, so we assert by replaying StateChanged" - but the actual oracle was just "raise event after Dispose; if handler is still subscribed, `_ = InvokeAsync(StateHasChanged)` on disposed component throws via bUnit's disposed-renderer guard; no exception = unsubscribed". No reflection, no NSubstitute call-count check. **Fix patterns**: (a) DELETE the misleading comments per §3.1 deletion-exempt category - test name + assertion shape are often self-documenting; (b) REPLACE with an accurate ≤3-line comment describing the ACTUAL oracle (requires §3.1 ask_user approval for new comment); (c) RENAME the test to embed the mechanism: `Dispose_Unsubscribes_SoRaisingEventAfterDoesNotThrowOnDisposedRenderer`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: test comments that ACCURATELY describe the test mechanism (read the comment + read the body, confirm match); tests whose intent comment names a HIGH-LEVEL invariant rather than a specific mechanism. **Audit method**: in NEW test files, look for `// (We assert via\|Verify by\|via reflection\|via subscriber count\|via call count\|reflection-based)` comment patterns. For each match, scan the same test method body for the named API. If absent, flag. |  | LOW |
| friend-grant-or-keep-public-when-di-seam-available | review-pass-only | {} | For every commit in the diff that either (a) adds an `[InternalsVisibleTo("<asm>")]` entry to a `.csproj` / `AssemblyInfo.cs` / equivalent in another language, OR (b) keeps a new type `public` whose cross-asm consumers all access it through a DI container (detect via: same diff or surrounding files contain DI registrar invocations - `services.Add...`, `ContainerBuilder.Register...`, `module { single { ... } }`, `bind(...).to(...)`, etc.): verify whether the DI-SEAM-EXTRACTION rung from `least-privilege-audit/axis-1-type-access.md` applies. The DI-seam pattern: extract a public DI registration extension on the owning assembly that registers the impl as `internal sealed` (with a public constructor for DI activator-based construction) with the appropriate lifetime using an idempotent registrar (`.TryAddSingleton`/`.TryAddScoped`/`.TryAddTransient`); cross-asm callers consume the extension + `IXxx` interface (or `IXxxFactory.Create(runtimeArg)`) via constructor injection. **High-signal failures**: diff adds an IVT entry whose justification is "the runtime needs to construct the impl class" - DI-seam is preferred; diff adds an IVT entry to expose 1-3 specific internal types when those types could be registered via a public DI extension instead; diff promotes a type to `public` solely so a runtime/host project can `new` it directly when the consumer already has a DI container in scope. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: IVT for test projects (`InternalsVisibleTo("MyProject.Tests")`); IVT for source generators or analyzers; IVT to expose a type constructed in startup-bootstrap code that runs BEFORE the DI container is built; **public SDK / NuGet / npm package surface** (per G6 conservative default - `<PackageId>` set, `<IsPackable>true</IsPackable>`, or PublicAPI baseline lists the type); **framework-mandated public types** (Razor components, Fluxor reducers, Spring beans, EF Core entity proxies, ASP.NET Core middleware); **reflection-discovered types** (EF Core entity types, `System.Text.Json` DTOs, attribute-discovered handlers, source-generator targets); **plugin / extension discovery types** where the publicness is itself the intended extension point; **mixed DI / non-DI consumers** (if any cross-asm consumer is non-DI-amenable - static initializer, module-level startup, etc. - DI-seam doesn't apply, KEEP-PUBLIC or CO-LOCATION is correct); KEEP-PUBLIC for record-style DTOs / request-response contracts that don't have a single implementation to hide. **Audit method**: scan diff for `[InternalsVisibleTo("...")]` additions AND new public classes. For each, detect whether DI registration patterns are present in the same diff or sibling files (`services.Add...`, equivalent in target ecosystem). If yes AND no carve-out applies (above list), flag with the DI-seam-extraction recommendation. Avoid name-vibes-only detection (e.g., "ends in Service/Factory/Manager") - use the structural DI-presence signal as the primary trigger. |  | LOW |
| committed-gate-skipped-or-unilaterally-carved-out | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** Review THIS session's branch + conversation (no artifact records a verbal promise, so anchor to what the panel sees) for the three momentum-driven gate-evasion shapes: (1) GATE-SKIP - the agent stated or committed a gate (a panel / review / design / approval owed for a scope) then performed worktree edits for that scope WITHOUT satisfying it; (2) UNILATERAL CARVE-OUT - the agent self-decided that part of a scope was exempt from a committed gate ("X is independent so it can go first") WITHOUT an ask_user; (3) ARTIFACT/SCOPE-SHIFT-WITHOUT-RE-GATE - a panel / cert that bound to artifact A had its authority carried to a NEW or shifted artifact B (including the PRE-EDIT SENTINEL not emitted before the first edit on B). **Why this matters**: these are the recurring honor-system failures - a committed gate skipped under momentum, a convenience carve-out the user never agreed to, a stale cert reused after a scope shift. HIGH-tier forces a per-commit core_rules_acknowledged enumeration (a recall lift; honest ceiling: DETECTIVE and self-reported at commit time, NOT deterministic teeth - no script reads a verbal promise). **High-signal failures**: a "panel before implementing" / "design first" commitment in the conversation followed by worktree edits with no panel between them; a per-scope exemption decided without ask_user; edits to a gate-bound artifact with no PRE-EDIT SENTINEL and no cert bound to THAT artifact. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: reading / investigating (not editing) before a gate; a carve-out explicitly approved via ask_user; a fresh re-gate (panel / cert) performed on the shifted artifact. **Audit method**: scan the conversation for committed gates, then the branch for edits that preceded their satisfaction; for each scope edited, confirm a bound cert and (for the first edit) a PRE-EDIT SENTINEL. Track in `panel-misses.csv`. |  | HIGH |
| input-edge-case-enumeration | review-pass-only | {} | For every CHANGED entry point in the diff that accepts input (function / method parameters, environment variables, file arguments, request fields, stdin, CLI args): enumerate EACH input surface against the four edge states {absent / not-provided, empty-string, whitespace-only, present-valid} and verify the resulting behavior is deliberate - fail-closed (rejected / errors) where a strict contract is intended, or documented-fail-open where empty is a valid value. **Why this matters**: the recurring miss is a present-but-EMPTY input (an explicitly-supplied empty / whitespace argument, or a set-but-empty environment variable) silently treated as "absent" and skipped, weakening a strict single-source / fail-closed contract and dropping the very input the caller meant to supply. **High-signal failures**: a string param guarded by truthiness (`if ($x)` / `if (x)`) so an explicit empty value falls through to a default; an env var read with `IsNullOrEmpty` then ignored when empty while a sibling source is silently used; a path / identifier accepted empty then passed downstream; a count / size read without a zero or negative check; an OS / platform variable used without a version guard. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: an edge state that is provably unreachable (cite why); a fail-open whose emptiness is the documented intent (an empty content body that is legitimately scannable-as-empty - cite the contract); inputs already validated by an upstream layer (trace the lineage). Do NOT sample one input - enumerate EACH input surface the diff adds or modifies. **Audit method**: list every input surface a changed entry point reads; for each, state the {absent \| empty \| whitespace \| present} behavior and confirm it is intended. Mnemonic: "every input x {absent \| empty \| whitespace \| present}." |  | HIGH |
| sibling-variant-consistency | review-pass-only | {} | For every change that touches ONE member of a set of N sibling constructs sharing a SHAPE (sibling regexes / validators / parsers / path handlers / format matchers / serializers that match the same input family): verify all siblings accept the SAME input VARIANTS - path separators (`/` vs `\`), letter case, word boundaries, Unicode vs ASCII forms, and both the empty and present forms. **Why this matters**: an odd-one-out sibling that handles only one variant (one pattern matching `foo/` while its siblings match `foo[\\/]`; one validator case-sensitive while its siblings are case-insensitive) is a silent bypass at exactly that member - a leak or escape that the per-member view misses because each member reads "fine" in isolation. **High-signal failures**: a workspace-path regex matching only the forward slash while sibling path regexes match both separators; a platform-specific path separator in one test so the intended case is not exercised on another OS; a parity check comparing only keys while a sibling compares mapped values; one create / update / rename path enforcing an invariant the others omit. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: a sibling whose distinct variant-handling is backed by a documented distinct contract (cite it); a member that genuinely operates on a different input family (not a true sibling). **Audit method**: grep the sibling set (the shared shape, or the entity noun + sibling verbs), diff each member's accepted-variant set, and flag the odd-one-out before declaring N/A. |  | HIGH |
| pr-creation-or-push-without-quality-gate-block | review-pass-only | {} | **PROCESS RULE.** At the PUBLISH boundary (`gh pr create`, `gh pr ready`, `gh api .../pulls` POST/PATCH, and every `git push` of a PR branch - including review-response pushes after a bot-fix), the agent MUST have run the per-PUBLISH gate: `gate-runner.ps1` / `gate-runner.sh` full-mode (which emits the §1B `QUALITY GATE` block) PLUS the whole-branch multi-model panel re-attestation (`panel-coverage: full-whole-branch`). The publish gate is DISTINCT from the per-COMMIT gate: `PRE-COMMIT GATE PASSED` + the per-commit `core_rules_acknowledged` enumeration governs `git commit`; the QUALITY GATE block + whole-branch panel coverage governs PR creation AND every review-response push. Running one does NOT satisfy the other. **Why this matters**: the recurring RCA is the agent rehearsing its per-commit slug-enumeration gate at the publish boundary - opening a PR (or pushing a review-response) with NO prior QUALITY GATE block - because the publish-gate spec was not in active context and nothing forces it at `gh pr create` (the tool is unhookable). HIGH-tier forces a per-publish QUALITY-GATE enumeration (a recall lift; honest ceiling: DETECTIVE + self-reported - `gh pr create` cannot be mechanically gated at create; the CI `quality-gate-check.yml` rg-floor is the merge-blocking remote backstop, but the QUALITY GATE block emission, the panel, and its verdict are AGENT-ASSERTED). **High-signal failures**: a `gh pr create` with no QUALITY GATE block earlier in the same turn / recent conversation; a review-response `git push` after addressing bot comments with no re-emitted whole-branch panel coverage (the per-commit ack is NOT the publish gate); substituting "tests green + per-commit `core_rules_acknowledged` done" for the publish gate; a publish that ran `gate-runner` in commit/triage mode instead of full-mode whole-branch. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: a publish tool call where a QUALITY GATE block (gate-runner full-mode ran, `gate_status: READY`) AND a whole-branch panel-coverage attestation appear since the branch tip / the most recent publish event; a `git push` of a NON-PR branch with no PR yet (the publish gate fires at the subsequent `gh pr create`); CI-internal or automation pushes that create no PR. **Audit method**: in any turn calling `gh pr create` / `gh pr ready` / `gh api` (pulls) / `git push` of a PR branch, verify a QUALITY GATE block (gate-runner full-mode, READY) plus the whole-branch panel-coverage attestation appear since the most recent publish event or the branch tip. If either is absent, the publish gate was bypassed. Process violation; track in `panel-misses.csv` under this slug. |  | HIGH |

## Patterns (lower-frequency, 3-4 hits in seed corpus - frequency label historical; inclusion is catalog-first per Tier semantics above)

| slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| js-interop-lifecycle | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"JSRuntime\\.InvokeAsync<IJSObjectReference>\|catch \\(JSException\|catch \\(JSDisconnectedException","glob":["*.cs","*.razor.cs"]} |  |  |
| internals-visibility | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"InternalsVisibleTo","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| image-link-broken | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"!\\[.*\\]\\(","glob":["*.md"]} |  |  |
| partial-file-cleanup | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"new\\s+(ProviderDbContext\|FileStream\|StreamWriter).*new","glob":["*.cs"]} |  |  |
| perf-batching | review-pass-only | {} | **Scope** (broader than the `perf-batching` name): covers hot-path repeated work, allocation, and materialization in the diff, not just batching. For every hot-path loop, render getter, callback, lookup, export/save path, or per-row operation in the diff, verify repeated work is batched, cached, single-pass, or streaming. **High-signal failures** (illustrative): per-row UI updates (`StateHasChanged`, `Progress<T>.Report`, `InvokeAsync(StateHasChanged)`, event-handler chains) inside large loops instead of batched/throttled updates; nested scans computing counts/membership by re-walking all entries for each tag/row/render; recomputing the same helper/delegate/property result inside a render predicate or markup expression when it can be cached for the current input; rebuilding a distinct/sorted list only to test membership where a `HashSet`/dictionary is enough; linearly scanning a dictionary/list in a per-disposal or cursor/index lookup when the key/id/rank is already available; recomputing keys/comparers/string projections for adjacent loop iterations instead of carrying the boundary value forward; buffering an entire potentially large stream/export into `ToList`/`ToArray`/`MemoryStream` when the consumer can iterate/write once. **C# hot-path allocation cross-check**: also scan for capturing lambdas, `Comparison<T>`/`IComparer<T>` construction, and LINQ/materialization inside methods called per row, per render, per selection/cursor operation, or per export chunk; if the code varies by sort/filter context, require caching by that context key or a documented cold-path reason. **Cede to the owning rule** (do not duplicate-flag): `repeated-collection-scan-in-click-handler` owns UI click/selection/command-handler scans of the selection's source collection, and `dictionary-ctor-over-linq-todictionary` owns `.ToDictionary(...)` where `new Dictionary(source)` suffices; perf-batching catches only the broader long tail those do not already surface. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: cold/admin/setup paths; tiny fixed-size collections; materialization needed for multiple passes, indexed access, a stable snapshot before mutation/concurrency, ordering needed by the UI, or a concrete-collection API boundary; per-item awaits where sequential semantics/backpressure are required. **Audit method**: enumerate every added/changed loop, LINQ chain, render getter/markup expression, event/disposal callback, index/cursor lookup, cache factory, and export/save path; for each, note its trigger frequency and input size; flag only when repeated work/allocation/materialization sits in a hot path AND you can name a concrete behavior-preserving same-scope alternative (hoist out of the loop, cache by the context key, single-pass, `HashSet`/dictionary lookup, stream/write-once) - if you cannot name the alternative, do not flag. |  |
| logic-inversion | review-pass-only | {} | For every early-return / short-circuit guard in modified `.cs`/`.razor.cs` files with predicates involving `!=`, `!`, `!=!`, or double-negatives: re-read the guard and verify it exits when state is the desired state, not when state is the OPPOSITE. Common bug: `if (_show != !isPinned) return;` - when `_show == !isPinned` (desired-state-already-set), this DOES exit; otherwise it doesn't. Verify the polarity matches intent. Ternary expressions returning the wrong branch (`condition ? badValue : goodValue`) and cross-method state flips where state is inverted twice and end up wrong are also caught here. |  |
| iasyncdisposable-fp | review-pass-only | {} | For every `await using` over a BCL type: verify the type implements `IAsyncDisposable` per Microsoft docs. Common FP: `ServiceProvider` (does implement both since .NET Core 3.0); `Stream` (does since .NET Core 3.0). Confirmed IDisposable-only: `SemaphoreSlim`, `HttpClient` (per .NET 8 docs). Cross-reference fp-2 below. | fp-2 |
| off-by-one-bounds | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW loop / index expression / range slice / `<= length` vs `< length` comparison / `array[length - N]` indexing / `Span.Slice(start, length)` added in the diff: verify the bounds are correct against the underlying collection size. **High-signal failures**: `for (int i = 0; i <= array.Length; i++)` - off by one (overruns); `array[array.Length - 1]` on an empty array - out of bounds; `text.Substring(start, length)` where `start + length > text.Length`; `Span.Slice(offset, count)` where `offset + count` overflows the span; pagination math `skip = (page - 1) * pageSize` with `page = 0`; `list.RemoveAt(0)` in a tight loop without re-checking `list.Count`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: bounds where the loop-invariant proves correctness AND the proof is documented in a one-line comment; pre-checked emptiness (`if (array.Length == 0) return; ... array[0]`); BCL methods that are documented to throw rather than corrupt (`ImmutableArray.Builder.Add` cannot overflow). Use the diff to find NEW bounds-sensitive operations; pre-existing patterns are not in scope. |  |
| input-validation-missing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW public method / public API endpoint / `[Parameter]` setter / `[HttpPost]` / `[HttpPut]` handler / event handler that takes untrusted input (`string`, byte[], collection, deserialized DTO) added in the diff: verify the input is validated at the entry point against length, format, range, allowed-value set, or other domain invariants. **High-signal failures**: HTTP endpoint accepts `string note` with no `[StringLength]` / `[MaxLength]` - DoS via gigabyte payload; method takes `int count` without `count > 0` check, leading to negative-loop math; takes `DateTime when` with no validation, allowing `DateTime.MinValue` propagation; takes `string filePath` consumed via `File.ReadAllBytes` with no validation. **Required defense**: `ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrEmpty(name)`, `ArgumentOutOfRangeException.ThrowIfNegativeOrZero(count)`, `[Required] [StringLength(...)]` attributes, custom domain validator at the entry point. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: internal methods consumed only by validated callers (trace the lineage); strongly-typed-id wrapper types whose constructor already validated (cross-reference `strongly-typed-id-preference`); inputs already validated by middleware (FluentValidation pipeline, model-binding [Required]). Use the diff to find NEW entry points; pre-existing unvalidated APIs in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| xss-injection-risk | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-controlled value rendered into HTML / JavaScript / SQL / shell context added in the diff: verify proper encoding/escaping/parameterization. **High-signal failures**: Razor `@((MarkupString)userInput)` - bypasses Blazor's default HTML encoding; `string sql = $"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{name}'";` - SQL injection via concatenation; `Process.Start("cmd.exe", $"/c echo {userInput}")` - command injection; `Response.WriteAsync($"<div>{userMessage}</div>")` - XSS via direct write without `HtmlEncoder`. **Required defenses**: `HtmlEncoder.Default.Encode(value)`, parameterized SQL (`SqlCommand.Parameters.Add`), shell-argument escaping (`Process.Start(new ProcessStartInfo { FileName = "cmd.exe", ArgumentList = { ... } })`), Blazor's default `@expression` encoding (do NOT use `@((MarkupString)...)` unless the source is already encoded). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: values from compile-time constants; values from an already-validated allow-list; values rendered into a context that explicitly tolerates the format (`<script>` JSON-stringified via `JsonSerializer.Serialize` - Blazor already handles); values run through a documented sanitization library. Use the diff to find NEW user-controlled render sites; pre-existing patterns in baseline are not in scope. |  |
| shell-script-discipline | diff-scoped | {"pattern":"^#!/(usr/)?bin/(ba)?sh","glob":["**/*.sh","**/.devcontainer/setup*"]} |  |  |
| code-duplication-dry | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW block of code added in the diff (>=5 lines): scan the diff and the surrounding files (within the same project / src tree) for substantively-identical blocks. **High-signal patterns**: same parameter assignment repeated across N method overloads (`SetA(...) { /* boilerplate */ Foo(x); } SetB(...) { /* same boilerplate */ Foo(y); }` - factor out the boilerplate); same try/catch/log block repeated across three different services; **same try/catch/exception-routing shape repeated across N≥2 methods OF THE SAME NEW FILE** (e.g., `DispatchSafelyAsync(Action)` and `ShowConfirmDialogAsync(Func<Task>)` both wrap an outer `try { await dispatch(() => { try { work } catch { route } }); } catch { route }` - extract a shared inner-catch helper `SafeInvoke(Action)` / `SafeInvokeAsync(Func<Task>)` so the outer dispatch only owns the dispatch-level catch); same validation-then-throw sequence in three constructors; same DTO mapping repeated across read/write methods. Flag the duplication and propose: (a) shared private helper, (b) base-class template method, (c) generic parameterization, (d) source-generator if the pattern is highly mechanical. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: duplication that aids isolation (e.g., test fixtures intentionally repeated to avoid cross-test coupling); duplication that's likely to diverge soon (single point of variation pending); duplication across security-boundary or assembly-boundary where coupling them would be worse; 5-line "duplications" that are actually just identical boilerplate signatures (`public override void Dispose() => ...`) without shared logic. **REPEAT-MISS DISCIPLINE (this duplication class has been missed 2x in review history - a `ShowModalAsync` helper + an `ShowConfirmDialogAsync`)**: when a NEW file contains N≥2 methods with try/catch shapes, panel slots MUST explicitly enumerate them by name and compare shapes before declaring CODE_REVIEW_READY. Cross-reference: §2C `DRY remediation gate` covers commit-time DRY audit; this rule covers in-PR-review-time DRY observation. Use the diff to find NEW blocks; baseline duplications are out of scope (separate refactor work). |  |
| ui-verb-terminology-inconsistency-across-surfaces | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified user-facing action verb in the diff that names the SAME logical operation across multiple UI surfaces (e.g., a button label + a context-menu item + a confirmation-dialog title + a toast message + an aria-label all referring to the same "remove this database" action): verify the verb is consistent across all surfaces. Mixing verbs ("Delete" on the button vs "Remove" in the context menu vs "Discard" in the confirmation) confuses users who don't realize the three labels describe the same action. **High-signal failure**: `ManageItemsTab.razor:111` bulk action button reads "Delete N" while the row context-menu item reads "Remove" and the confirmation dialog says "Are you sure you want to remove {fileName}?". Three verbs for one action. **Fix patterns**: (a) pick ONE verb per action (project-level convention is usually "Remove" for non-destructive UI removal, "Delete" for destructive); (b) audit ALL surfaces in the diff that name the action - button label, menu item, confirmation title, confirmation body, toast/banner success message, toast/banner error message, aria-label, telemetry event name - and confirm they all use the same verb; (c) extract to a constants file (`UiActionLabels.Remove`) if the same string appears 3+ times. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: cases where two surfaces describe different aspects of the action (e.g., "Remove" for the removal step + "Cleanup" for the post-removal disposal - different operations); UI that follows a documented project convention deviating from this rule (cite the convention in the PR description). **Audit method**: for any NEW user-action UI element in the diff, search the diff for OTHER references to the same action by data-flow (same `@onclick` handler, same `EventCallback`, same Razor `case ActionKind.X:`). Compare the verb across each reference; flag inconsistencies. |  | MEDIUM |
| prefers-reduced-motion-not-extended-to-new-transition | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW CSS rule in the diff that adds an `animation` / `transition` / `transform` property (any non-`none` value) to an element class: verify the SAME stylesheet's existing `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` override block (if one exists) ALSO lists the new class. Bot flagged twice across 2 rounds; user with reduced-motion preference still sees the slide-in animation. **Fix patterns**: (a) add the new class to the existing reduced-motion selector list: `.db-entry-row, .db-entry-upgrading, ..., .db-entry-checkbox { animation: none; transition: none; }`; (b) use a wildcard / attribute-based override at the top-level (`@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { *, *::before, *::after { animation-duration: 0.01ms !important; transition-duration: 0.01ms !important; } }` - opinionated but covers everything); (c) extract a CSS custom property for transition duration and override the variable in the reduced-motion block. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: stylesheets without an existing `prefers-reduced-motion` block (no precedent to extend; the original file's accessibility-strategy choice was no-block - DON'T introduce one unilaterally); transitions on properties that don't cause perceptible motion (color-only transitions, opacity-fade-only); animations whose duration is already <100ms (below human perception threshold for vestibular response). **Audit method**: for every NEW CSS rule with `animation:` / `transition:` / `transform:` in modified `.css` / `.razor.css` files, grep the same file for `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)`. If found, verify the targeted class/selector appears in the reduced-motion override; if absent, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| extension-method-style-inconsistency-with-codebase | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW extension method declaration in the diff (`public static class Xyz` containing `public static <Type> Method(this <T> source, ...)` OR the newer C# 14 `extension(<T> source) { public <Type> Method(...) { } }` block syntax): verify the declaration style matches the CONVENTION used by sibling extension methods in the same project / namespace. Mixing the two syntaxes within a codebase creates visual noise + cognitive overhead for readers + complicates IDE refactoring. **High-signal failure**: `MenuHostDependenciesExtensions.cs` + `DatabaseToolsTestDependencies.cs` use the new C# 14 `extension(IServiceCollection services) { public void AddXxx(...) { ... } }` block syntax, but every other extension method in the same test project (`BannerHostDependenciesExtensions`, `ModalServiceDependenciesExtensions`, etc.) uses the traditional `public static class X { public static IServiceCollection AddXxx(this IServiceCollection services, ...) { ... } }` form. Bot flagged twice on consistency. **Fix patterns**: (a) use the traditional `public static class X { public static ... this T ... }` syntax to match existing siblings; (b) IF the project explicitly chose to migrate to C# 14 extension blocks, document the convention in `README.md` AND open follow-up PRs to convert siblings - DON'T leave the codebase in a half-migrated state; (c) ask via `ask_user` which convention the project is adopting before writing the first new extension method. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: net-new projects with no sibling extension methods (style is a fresh decision); projects whose `README.md` documents an in-flight migration; first extension method introduced into a project where the language version was just bumped to support the new syntax (with an `ask_user` confirmation). **Audit method**: for every NEW extension-method declaration in the diff, grep the same project / namespace for OTHER `(this \w+\s+\w+)` patterns (traditional) AND `extension\(\w+\s+\w+\)` patterns (C# 14). If both exist, the project is in a mixed state - flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| empty-collection-default-comparer-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW property / parameter / field of type `FrozenSet<TKey>` / `FrozenDictionary<TKey, TValue>` / `HashSet<TKey>` / `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` / `ImmutableHashSet<TKey>` / `ImmutableSortedDictionary<TKey, TValue>` in the diff that uses a default empty-collection value (e.g., `FrozenSet<string>.Empty`, `ImmutableHashSet<string>.Empty`): verify the default's comparer matches the comparer used by consumers that read into the collection. The static `.Empty` instances are created with the type's DEFAULT comparer - for `FrozenSet<string>.Empty` that's `EqualityComparer<string>.Default` (case-sensitive `Ordinal` comparison). If consumers do case-insensitive lookups via `OrdinalIgnoreCase` (common pattern for file names, header names, identifier names), the empty default is INCOMPATIBLE - silent semantic mismatch that surfaces only when callers add elements with one comparer and read them with another. **High-signal failure**: `BannerProgressEntry.cs:23` declared `public required FrozenSet<string> BatchFileNames { get; init; } = FrozenSet<string>.Empty;` while the rest of the codebase looks up file names case-insensitively via `OrdinalIgnoreCase`. Default-empty instances would behave inconsistently with non-empty instances created via `fileNames.ToFrozenSet(StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)`. **Fix patterns**: (a) use a domain-specific static field defaulting with the right comparer: `private static readonly FrozenSet<string> EmptyOrdinalIgnoreCase = FrozenSet.ToFrozenSet(Array.Empty<string>(), StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase); public required FrozenSet<string> BatchFileNames { get; init; } = EmptyOrdinalIgnoreCase;`; (b) require callers to always populate the collection (drop the default, use `required`); (c) for `ImmutableHashSet`/`ImmutableSortedSet`, use `ImmutableHashSet.Create<string>(StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)` as the default. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: collections whose consumers genuinely use default comparison (case-sensitive `Ordinal` for unique-id strings, byte-sequence content, etc.); collections where the type is `IReadOnlyCollection<T>` / `IEnumerable<T>` (no comparer surface); collections that are guaranteed-empty-and-never-read (e.g., a stub used only as a sentinel). **Audit method**: for every NEW collection property / parameter typed as `FrozenSet<T>` / `FrozenDictionary` / `HashSet` / `Dictionary` / `ImmutableHashSet` / `ImmutableSortedDictionary` with a default-empty value, grep for adjacent code that POPULATES the same collection type via `.ToFrozenSet(StringComparer.X)` or similar. If the populated path specifies a non-default comparer, the default-empty path is mismatched. |  | MEDIUM |
| registry-stack-active-change-no-state-replay | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified stack-based observer registry / multi-host coordinator in the diff (registries that track an "active" item from a stack of registered items, e.g., `IMenuHostRegistry`, `IModalCoordinator` with a host stack, multi-instance singleton routers): verify the moment the ACTIVE item changes (push, pop, swap), the newly-active item receives a state-snapshot / catch-up event covering the state that was already active when it became the new active. Without this, the newly-active observer enters a "stale" state where it doesn't know about pre-existing condition (e.g., a menu was already open when this host became the topmost; the host renders no menu because it never got the open-state event). **High-signal failure**: `MenuHost.razor.cs:115` - when `MenuService.ActiveItems != null` (a menu IS open) and the active `MenuHost` changes via `MenuHostRegistry.SetActive(newHost)`, the newly-active host never receives a `MenuService.StateChanged` event. `_focusedMenuId` remains stale; the new host renders an empty menu area while a menu is conceptually open. **Fix patterns**: (a) on registry `SetActive(newHost)`, immediately invoke a state-replay callback on `newHost` (`newHost.OnStateRehydrate(currentSnapshot)`); (b) subscribe the registry to the underlying state-source and re-broadcast on every active-change AS WELL as state-change; (c) have the newly-active item proactively pull state on its `OnActivated` lifecycle hook (pull-based rehydrate); (d) document the eventual-consistency window if state-replay is genuinely too expensive. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: registries whose state is genuinely stateless (no relevant cross-active state to replay); registries where active-change is RARE and a single missed initial render is acceptable; registries with a single active item per session lifetime (no replay scenario). **Audit method**: for every NEW registry / coordinator class in the diff that has a `SetActive`/`Register`/`Push`-style method AND an associated state-source, view the `SetActive` body. If it does NOT invoke a state-replay on the new active item, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| repeated-collection-scan-in-click-handler | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified UI click handler / event handler / command handler in the diff that operates on a user-selection set: verify the handler does NOT scan the underlying source collection (`Entries`, `Items`, `_dataSource`) multiple times via `FirstOrDefault` / `Where` / repeated `foreach` loops AND additionally call helpers that re-scan internally. Repeated scans turn an O(N) operation into O(N²) or worse for large selection counts; user-perceived UI latency on bulk operations. **High-signal failure**: `ManageItemsTab.razor.cs:762` `OnBulkActionClickAsync` does `foreach (var selected in _selectedForRemoval) { var entry = OrderService.Entries.FirstOrDefault(e => e.FileName == selected); ... }` AND `IsUpgradeEligibleForSelected()` (called separately) ALSO scans `OrderService.Entries`. With 200 databases selected, that's 200 * 200 = 40000 comparisons just for the eligibility check. **Fix patterns**: (a) build a `Dictionary<string, DatabaseEntry>` snapshot ONCE at handler start, then look up each selection by O(1) key access; (b) use `Lookup<string, DatabaseEntry>` if multiple entries can share a key; (c) pre-filter the source collection ONCE into a smaller collection of selected entries, then operate on that; (d) push the eligibility check into the source so it runs against an indexed view. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: handlers operating on small fixed-size selections (≤ 5 items by design); handlers where the scan happens once + cached at handler start; handlers where the source collection itself is already a `Dictionary` / `Lookup` / indexed view. **Audit method**: for every NEW click/event handler in the diff, count the distinct `.FirstOrDefault(`, `.Single(`, `.Where(`, `foreach (var x in <sourceCollection>)` invocations against the SAME source. If ≥2 against the same source, AND the source's expected size is unbounded user data, flag with O(N²) warning. |  | MEDIUM |
| scope-state-not-reset-on-tab-restart-or-rerun | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified component / service / tab / workflow in the diff that supports a "restart" / "re-run" / "reset" / "new-session" lifecycle hook AND tracks per-session state in instance fields (counters, `_lastRenderedCount`, `_processedIds`, `_seenFiles`, sticky flags): verify EVERY tracked state field is reset to its initial value on the lifecycle restart, OR is explicitly documented as cross-session-cumulative. When the tab starts a NEW run, it resets `Entries` to empty but `_lastRenderedCount` remains at the previous run's count. Subsequent entries (indices 1..N) never satisfy `Entries.Count > _lastRenderedCount` (because N < previous count) - auto-scroll never fires for the new run. **Fix patterns**: (a) reset every per-session state field in the restart method: `private void StartNewRun() { Entries = []; _lastRenderedCount = 0; _processedIds.Clear(); _hasErrored = false; ... }`; (b) extract per-session state into a `SessionState` record that gets re-created on restart (impossible to forget a field); (c) introduce a `[SessionScoped]` marker attribute + reflection-based reset (overkill for most cases but works for very large state surfaces). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: state that's genuinely cumulative across runs (total-since-startup counters - document with `_cumulativeTotal` naming); state whose reset is handled by re-creating the entire component instance (parent re-renders the child); state explicitly tied to the parent / outer lifetime. **Audit method**: for every NEW or modified instance field in a "restartable" component / service, grep the same class for the restart/reset method. If the field is NOT assigned in the restart method AND is NOT documented as cumulative, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| js-interop-module-attach-without-detach-lifecycle | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW JS module / IJSObjectReference in the diff that registers a listener / observer / interval / DOM-event-handler / subscription in an `attach()` / `init()` / `register()` function: verify the module ALSO exports a corresponding `detach()` / `dispose()` / `unregister()` function AND that the corresponding `.razor.cs` or consumer calls it from `DisposeAsync` / `Dispose`. Listeners registered without detach LEAK on component disposal - the JS event handler holds a reference to the .NET callback (`DotNetObjectReference`), which keeps the .NET component alive past intended disposal, leading to "method invoked on disposed instance" exceptions in the JS handler. **High-signal failure**: `DatabaseToolsLogView.js:18` exports `attach()` which calls `addEventListener('scroll', handler)` but exports no `detach()`. The corresponding `DatabaseToolsLogView.razor.cs.DisposeAsync` has no way to remove the listener. Each modal open/close leaks a scroll-handler holding a `DotNetObjectReference`. **Fix patterns**: (a) export a paired `detach()` function: `export function attach(...) { ... ; return () => detachHandler(); }` returning a cleanup callback; (b) export named `detach()`: `export function detach() { element.removeEventListener('scroll', handler); }`; (c) wire detach into the component's `DisposeAsync`: `await _jsModule.InvokeVoidAsync("detach"); _jsModule.DisposeAsync();`; (d) use `AddEventListener` with `signal: abortController.signal` and abort on disposal (modern web platform - single call cleans up multiple listeners). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: JS modules that register listeners on the WINDOW or DOCUMENT (not component-scoped) and are intentionally lifetime-of-page; JS modules whose listeners are stateless and idempotent (re-firing is harmless, no .NET callback held); listeners registered on elements that themselves get garbage-collected when the component unmounts (rare - verify). **Audit method**: for every NEW JS module file in the diff (`.js` / `.razor.js`), grep for `addEventListener\|setInterval\|MutationObserver\|new IntersectionObserver\|new ResizeObserver\|Reflect.has`. For each match, grep the same file for a corresponding `removeEventListener\|clearInterval\|.disconnect()\|.abort()`. If missing, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| pluralization-singular-plural-grammar-mismatch | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW user-facing string in the diff that interpolates a count + a noun + a verb form (`$"{count} database needs attention"`, `$"{count} file was imported"`, `$"{n} item is selected"`): verify the verb agrees with the singular-vs-plural form of the count. `count == 1` requires singular verb ("needs", "was", "is", "has"); `count != 1` requires plural verb ("need", "were", "are", "have"). "" : "s")} need attention"` - pluralizes "database/databases" correctly but uses singular-form "need" always. "needs" : "need")} attention"`; (b) use a helper method `Pluralize(count, "database", "databases", "needs", "need")`; (c) restructure to avoid the count + verb pair: `$"{(count == 1 ? "1 database needs" : $"{count} databases need")} attention"`; (d) use a localization library that handles plural rules (ICU MessageFormat, `IStringLocalizer<T>.GetString` with `pluralForm` parameter). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: count interpolations with no following verb ("3 results"); strings that ONLY ever fire for count > 1 (verify by inspecting the call site's guard); strings whose verb form is invariant ("count failed: {count}" - no verb agreement needed). **Audit method**: for every NEW string interpolation in the diff containing `\b\d+\b` or `{\w+}` + `(was\|were\|is\|are\|has\|have\|need\|needs\|do\|does)\b`, verify the verb's plural-form is conditional on the count. If the verb is hardcoded to one form, flag. |  | MEDIUM |
| strongly-typed-id-none-rejection | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW method in the diff that accepts a strongly-typed ID parameter (`UserId`, `EntryId`, `SessionId` etc.) and uses it to look up / match / route to a target: verify the method explicitly rejects the sentinel `None`/`Empty`/`Default(T)` value AT METHOD ENTRY. Count : throw new ArgumentException(); }` enforces on primary-constructor calls - but `someRecord with { Count = -1 }` bypasses the constructor and lands a -1. **High-signal failure**: `PageSize` record with primary constructor validating `> 0` but caller did `existing with { PageSize = 0 }` and the resulting record carried 0 past the validation gate. **Fix patterns**: (a) duplicate the invariant in the property initializer: `public required int Count { get; init; } = ValidateCount(Count);` (works for `with` too, because `init` setter runs the validator); (b) make the property fully-`get`-only (drop `init`) - `with`-expression cannot then mutate; (c) document the invariant-bypass risk in XML doc AND ensure no code path uses `with`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: records with no invariants beyond null-check; records whose `init` setters explicitly re-run the same validator that the primary constructor used; records intentionally allowing invariant violation for builder-pattern progressive construction (document the staging contract). **Audit method**: for every NEW record / class with a primary constructor doing validation, check whether any property uses `init` (or `set`). | stale-read-fix-coverage-completeness | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW fix in the diff that addresses a stale-read bug (race between writer and reader; cache-eviction visibility delay): verify the fix is applied to ALL methods that read the same field, not just the reader that surfaced the bug. | pr-description-api-surface-sync | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW PR description content that names a public API surface (type kind: "introduces a record / class / struct / interface", method signature, parameter list, return type): verify the description matches the actual implementation.
