this rule owns SERIALIZATION TYPE-discriminator back-compat across a rename for persisted / exported payloads. **Audit method**: enumerate changed serialization discriminators, converter kind-switches, and persisted-by-name enums; for each renamed value, flag-for-manual when no legacy-value acceptance or migration covers persisted / exported payloads, burden-shifting to the author to cite the back-compat path or prove the data never persists or crosses versions. | | MEDIUM |
| rid-platform-architecture-signals-must-agree | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified MSBuild architecture signal in the diff (`RuntimeIdentifier` / `RuntimeIdentifiers`, `Platform` / `Platforms`, `PlatformTarget`, `AppxBundlePlatforms`, a native or architecture-specific `PackageReference` or `runtimes/(rid)/native` asset, or a `-r` / `--arch` / `--runtime` publish argument in a script or CI workflow): verify ALL of them resolve to the SAME target architecture for every build and publish path (each configuration, each CI leg), so the build cannot emit an artifact whose managed PlatformTarget and native architecture disagree. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a `.csproj` pinning `PlatformTarget` to x64 while a CI publish leg passes `-r win-arm64`, or an arm64 `RuntimeIdentifier` paired with an x64-only native package - the build emits an artifact whose managed PlatformTarget and native architecture disagree, faulting at load on the target device. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: a single source-of-truth property drives every signal (cite the property); an intentionally architecture-neutral build (`AnyCPU` with no native-arch dependency) documented as such; the mismatch is confined to a non-shipping / test-only leg that never produces a shipped artifact. **Cede to the owning rule**: `doc-impl-mismatch` owns prose / README / doc claims that disagree with the build; this rule owns the cross-signal ARCHITECTURE AGREEMENT among RID, Platform, PlatformTarget, and native-asset selection for every build and publish path. **Audit method**: enumerate changed architecture signals across `.csproj` / `.props` / `.targets` / CI workflows / publish scripts; for each build and publish path, flag-for-manual when the signals do not all resolve to one architecture, burden-shifting to the author to show the single-architecture resolution for each path. | | MEDIUM |
| collection-copyto-prevalidates-destination | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW `ICollection<T>.CopyTo` / `ICollection.CopyTo` / `CopyTo(Array, int)` implementation added in the diff: verify it validates the destination (array not null, index not negative, and array length minus index is at least the element count) BEFORE writing any element, so an invalid call throws the documented `ArgumentNullException` / `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` / `ArgumentException` WITHOUT partially mutating the destination. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a custom collection's `CopyTo(T[] array, int arrayIndex)` that loops `array[arrayIndex + i] = item` with no upfront capacity check - a too-small array throws `IndexOutOfRangeException` MIDWAY through, leaving the destination partially overwritten (and the wrong exception type versus the documented `ArgumentException`). **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the implementation delegates to a BCL method that already prevalidates (`List<T>.CopyTo`, `Array.Copy` with an upfront length check - cite it); a documented internal-only collection whose single caller pre-checks capacity (cite it); the implementation already throws the correct argument exceptions before any write. **Cede to the owning rule**: `input-validation-missing` owns missing validation of untrusted input at new public entry points; `debug-assert-not-runtime-validation` owns Debug/Conditional asserts used as release-vanishing validation of external input outside the CopyTo contract; this rule owns the `ICollection.CopyTo` CONTRACT precondition - validate the destination before mutating it, including when the attempted check is written only as a `Debug.Assert`. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW `CopyTo` implementations; verify a pre-loop guard (array-null, index-range, and remaining-capacity) precedes any element write; flag when the write loop can begin before the capacity check, and flag-for-manual when the prevalidation is claimed to live in a delegated call (require the author to cite the delegate that prevalidates). | | MEDIUM |
| os-gated-batch-size-or-fallback-for-platform-limits | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified call in the diff that passes a BATCH SIZE / count / buffer capacity / page size to a platform or OS API that DOCUMENTS a LOWER limit on some supported OS versions (an older Windows build, a down-level API surface): verify the size is chosen by OS capability (`OperatingSystem.IsWindowsVersionAtLeast(...)`, a `RuntimeInformation` probe, or a documented capability check) OR the call retries with a safe smaller fallback on the limit error, so the call does not fail on the LOWEST supported OS. **High-signal failure**: PR-era passing a large `EvtNext` batch count that exceeds the documented limit on an older supported Windows version - on that OS the call fails or silently returns fewer items, and there is no capability gate or fallback-retry guarding the down-level path. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the batch size is within the documented limit for EVERY supported OS (cite the floor); the app's minimum supported OS / TFM is above the version where the limit applies (cite the min-version); a fallback retry on the limit error is already present. **Cede to the owning rule**: `silent-fallback-loses-info` owns a fallback that drops the failure cause without reporting it; `input-edge-case-enumeration` owns enumerating input edge states (absent / empty / whitespace / present-valid), including a generic count or an OS / platform env-var or config input lacking a guard; this rule owns CHOOSING the batch size by OS capability, or retrying with a safe smaller fallback, for a documented platform-version-gated API limit. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW platform-API calls passing a batch / count / capacity argument; for each whose API documents an OS-version-gated lower limit, flag-for-manual when neither an OS-capability gate nor a fallback retry guards the lowest supported OS, burden-shifting to the author to cite the per-OS limit floor or the guard. | | MEDIUM |
| failure-outcome-diagnostics-preserve-specific-cause | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified handler in the diff for a FAILURE / TIMEOUT / CANCELLATION / protocol-mismatch / deserialization-failure / safety-breaker / capped-buffer / race path that produces a REPORTED OUTCOME (a returned `Result` / status enum / error object, a thrown exception, a dispatched failure action, or a structured logged outcome a caller consumes): verify the outcome preserves the SPECIFIC CAUSE (which failure occurred), the TERMINAL STATE (cancelled vs timed-out vs faulted vs truncated vs rejected), and the ORIGINATING TOKEN / id / correlation, rather than collapsing distinct failures into one generic value a caller cannot disambiguate. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a timeout-vs-cancellation race reporting a single `OperationCanceledException` for both, so the caller cannot tell a real timeout from a user cancel; a capped-buffer read returning a "success" outcome with silently-truncated data instead of a "truncated" terminal state; a deserialization failure collapsed into a generic `false` / `null` that drops which field or schema version mismatched; a cancellation whose reported outcome carries a different (or default) token than the one that was cancelled. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the distinct causes are genuinely indistinguishable to EVERY caller (cite that no caller branches on the difference); a path with only one possible failure mode; the cause and terminal state are preserved in a structured outcome the caller can consume (cite it). **Cede to the owning rule**: `silent-fallback-loses-info` owns a fallback that returns a default without reporting the cause at all; `facade-discards-inner-result-discriminator` owns a WRAPPER collapsing an inner result-discriminator to bool; `message-asserts-outcome-not-guaranteed-on-every-branch` owns a user-facing or log MESSAGE STRING that asserts an outcome; `callback-swallows-exception-the-caller-expects-to-propagate` owns a callback that swallows an exception the caller expects to propagate; `silent-cancellation-via-early-return-vs-throw` owns a cancellation that silently returns instead of throwing (the caller cannot tell complete from cancelled); `await-throwable-async-call-without-handler-in-teardown-flow` owns a missing or too-narrow catch around an awaited teardown / disconnect / focus call; `partial-cleanup-on-async-failure-path` owns incomplete resource or token cleanup on an async failure path; `failure-detail-collected-but-not-surfaced` owns a loop that collects per-item failures but announces only a count; `sentinel-artifact-guard-covers-all-retry-states` owns the state-combination coverage of a stale-sentinel retry guard; this rule owns the STRUCTURED reported outcome of a failure / timeout / cancellation handler under-discriminating the specific cause-TYPE, the terminal state (cancel vs timeout vs faulted vs truncated vs rejected), or the originating token when a caller branches on the difference. **Audit method**: enumerate changed failure / timeout / cancellation / deserialization / breaker / capped-buffer handlers; for each, identify the distinct causes and terminal states a caller may need to distinguish; flag-for-manual when the reported outcome collapses them into one generic value AND a caller branches on the difference, burden-shifting to the author to cite caller-indistinguishability or the preserved cause / terminal-state / token. | | MEDIUM |
| user-action-side-effects-after-commit-boundary | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified user-initiated operation in the diff (a save / delete / apply / submit / import / export flow) that performs SIDE EFFECTS - showing progress, clearing or resetting existing user state, honoring a cancellation, or publishing intermediate state - verify those side effects do NOT fire BEFORE the user has COMMITTED the operation (confirmed / clicked apply / passed the point of intent) AND are not honored AFTER the operation has crossed its IRREVERSIBLE COMMIT BOUNDARY (the point past which a cancel can no longer cleanly undo). A user who has not committed, or who cancels before the boundary, must not see existing state cleared, progress shown, or partial effects applied; a cancel requested after the boundary must not half-undo a committed operation. **High-signal failure**: PR-era clearing the current filter or list the moment a dialog OPENS (before the user clicks Apply), so cancelling leaves the user with cleared state they never asked to lose; honoring a cancel after the write has already begun, leaving a half-written artifact; showing a "Saved" / progress side effect before the commit actually succeeds. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the side effect IS the committed action (cite it); an explicitly two-phase / preview UI where the clear or progress is documented and fully reversible on cancel; an operation with no irreversible boundary (every step is undoable). **Cede to the owning rule**: `idempotent-no-op-actions-preserve-user-state` owns a reducer preserving adjacent user state when an already-active value is re-requested; `message-asserts-outcome-not-guaranteed-on-every-branch` owns a user-facing or log MESSAGE STRING that asserts an outcome (e.g. a premature "Saved"); this rule owns deferring user-action side effects (progress / clear / cancellation) until the user has committed and before the operation crosses its irreversible commit boundary. **Audit method**: enumerate changed user-initiated operations that perform side effects; for each, identify the commit point and the irreversible boundary; flag-for-manual when a side effect can fire before commit, or a cancellation is honored after the boundary, burden-shifting to the author to cite the commit gating or the reversibility of the side effect. | | MEDIUM |
| sentinel-artifact-guard-covers-all-retry-states | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified retry / recovery / resume path in the diff that is BLOCKED or GATED by a SENTINEL ARTIFACT (a leftover backup file, a lock file, a marker, a `.tmp` / `.partial` artifact, an in-progress flag): verify the guard checks the sentinel's staleness INDEPENDENTLY of the primary artifact's state, so a STALE sentinel left by a crashed prior run does not permanently block the retry, while a VALID sentinel from a live run still blocks. A guard that only handles one state combination (e.g. proceeds only when BOTH the primary is absent AND the sentinel is absent) leaves the crash-leftover combinations unhandled. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a retry that proceeds only when the primary artifact is absent AND the lock file is absent - a crash that leaves the lock file but no primary artifact permanently blocks every retry; a recovery that checks only the primary-present state and ignores a stale `.partial` backup that a later step then trips over. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the sentinel and primary are genuinely coupled so their states cannot diverge (cite why); a sentinel that already carries a TTL / timestamp / owner-pid staleness check; a single-state guard documented as covering the only reachable combination. **Cede to the owning rule**: `partial-cleanup-on-async-failure-path` owns completing resource / token cleanup on an async failure path; this rule owns a stale sentinel / lock / marker / backup that BLOCKS a retry being checked independently of the primary artifact state across all crash-leftover combinations. **Audit method**: enumerate changed retry / resume / recovery guards gated by a sentinel / lock / marker / backup; for each, enumerate the crash-leftover state combinations (primary present or absent, sentinel present or absent); flag-for-manual when a stale-sentinel-without-primary (or the inverse) combination permanently blocks the retry, burden-shifting to the author to cite the staleness check or the primary-sentinel coupling. | | MEDIUM |
| null-progress-means-no-progress-sink | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW code path in the diff that allocates a no-op or empty progress sink (a `Progress<T>` whose handler does nothing, a `NullProgress`, an empty `IProgress<T>` lambda) where the ESTABLISHED CONVENTION in the surrounding code is that a `null` progress argument already signals "the caller does not consume progress" (the callee guards reports with `progress?.Report(...)`): verify the path passes `null` (the established no-progress signal) instead of allocating a throwaway no-op sink, so sibling paths stay consistent and no needless allocation occurs. **High-signal failure**: PR-era one overload passes `null` for the no-progress case while a sibling allocates `new Progress<int>(_ => { })` for the same case - inconsistent and a wasted allocation, when downstream `progress?.Report(...)` already handles `null`. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the no-op sink is required because the callee does NOT null-check the progress argument (cite the unconditional `progress.Report`); the codebase convention is to always pass a non-null sink (cite it); the sink does real work and is not a no-op. **Cede to the owning rule**: `sibling-variant-consistency` owns a general odd-one-out across two or more sibling members; `perf-batching` owns repeated hot-path allocation and per-item perf impact; this rule owns the specific null-is-the-no-progress-signal convention versus allocating a no-op progress sink. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW progress-sink allocations; for each, check whether the surrounding convention uses `null` as the no-progress signal AND the callee guards with `progress?.Report`; flag a no-op sink allocated where `null` would serve, and flag-for-manual when the callee's null-handling is out of the diff (require the author to confirm the callee null-checks). | | LOW |
| loop-over-recursive-input-skips | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW recursive method in the diff that SKIPS repeated ignorable / filler / no-op input records (whitespace, comments, padding, keep-alive frames, ignorable tokens) from a pipe / stream / parser / network / other UNTRUSTED-LENGTH source by CALLING ITSELF to advance past each skipped record: verify it uses a LOOP instead, because an adversarial or pathological input with a long run of skippable records recurses once per record and overflows the stack. **High-signal failure**: PR-era `ReadNext()` that, on encountering a skippable record, does `return ReadNext();` to advance - a stream of N skippable records recurses N frames deep; a malformed or adversarial input with thousands of them stack-overflows the process. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the recursion depth is provably bounded by a small constant (cite the bound); a tail call the runtime provably optimizes (rare on .NET - cite it); the input is trusted AND length-bounded before the recursion (cite the bound). **Cede to the owning rule**: `input-validation-missing` owns absent validation of untrusted input at a new entry point; `loop-silently-drops-failed-items` owns unsignalled data-loss skips from already-validated input; this rule owns converting a self-recursive skip-advance over untrusted-length input into a loop for stack safety. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW recursive methods that re-call themselves to skip or advance past ignorable input records; flag when the recursion count is bounded only by input length from an untrusted source, and flag-for-manual when the bound is claimed to live outside the diff (require the author to cite it). | | MEDIUM |
| evaluate-render-validators-once-per-value | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW render path in the diff (a Razor component render, a cell or row renderer, a template builder) that consults a CALLER-PROVIDED validation / predicate / formatter MORE THAN ONCE for the SAME value within a single render (e.g. once to decide a CSS class and again to decide the text or title): verify it computes the validation ONCE and reuses the result for that render, because the caller's validator may be expensive or stateful, so re-invoking it per render-use multiplies the cost across every rendered item (and risks divergence if the validator is non-deterministic). **High-signal failure**: PR-era a cell renderer calling `IsValid(value)` once for the `class` attribute and again for the `title`, doubling the caller's validation cost per cell across a large grid; a formatter invoked twice per row for the same value. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the validation is provably cheap AND pure (cite it); the two calls are for genuinely different values; a documented contract that the validator is idempotent and free of cost. **Cede to the owning rule**: `perf-batching` owns generic or pure repeated computation and per-item re-render in a render path; `defer-expensive-fallback-until-needed` owns eager load or IO of a rarely-needed fallback before the primary path fails; this rule owns RE-INVOKING a CALLER-PROVIDED validator, predicate, or formatter (whose cost or statefulness the component does not control) multiple times for the same value within a single render. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW render paths that call a caller-provided validation / predicate / formatter; for each value, count the invocations within one render; flag when the same value's validator is called more than once where one computation would serve, and flag-for-manual when the validator is claimed cheap-and-pure (require the author to cite it). | | MEDIUM |
| defer-expensive-fallback-until-needed | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW code path in the diff that EAGERLY loads, computes, or does IO for a FALLBACK (a default resource, a secondary data source, a backup lookup table, a fallback parser or formatter) BEFORE the primary path is known to have failed: verify the expensive fallback work is DEFERRED until the later parser / formatter / consumer actually needs it, because the fallback is rarely reached and eager loading pays its full cost on every call. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a formatter that loads a large fallback message table at construction even though the table is only consulted when the primary lookup misses (rare); an eager `File.ReadAllBytes` of a fallback resource on every parse when the primary path almost always succeeds. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the fallback is cheap to load (cite it); the fallback is consulted on the common path, not rarely; a documented warm-up or pre-load requirement makes eager loading intentional (cite it). **Cede to the owning rule**: `silent-fallback-loses-info` owns a fallback that returns a default without reporting the cause; `perf-batching` owns generic hot-path repeated work or materialization; this rule owns DEFERRING the expensive load or IO of a rarely-needed fallback until it is actually consumed. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW fallback loads or computations; for each, determine whether the primary path is the common case and the fallback is rarely reached; flag-for-manual when an expensive fallback is loaded eagerly before the primary is known to fail, burden-shifting to the author to cite the fallback's cheapness or its common-path need. | | MEDIUM |
| component-ref-cache-prunes-null-captures | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified cache or dictionary of component or element references in the diff (a `Dictionary<TKey, ElementReference>`, a map of `@ref` captures, a component-instance cache) that is PRUNED when its DOMAIN ITEM disappears: verify it ALSO prunes entries whose captured ref has been CLEARED to null (the component was disposed, re-rendered away, or its `@ref` reset), not only entries whose domain key is gone, so the cache does not retain stale null-ref entries that a later use dereferences or that leak. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a `Dictionary<Guid, ElementReference>` pruned only when the corresponding item is removed from the list, but an item that stays while its component is disposed (its ref captured null) leaves a stale null-ref entry that a later focus or scroll call dereferences. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the ref provably cannot be cleared while the domain item exists (cite the lifetime coupling); the cache is rebuilt fresh each render so no stale entry can persist; null-ref entries are tolerated and explicitly checked at the use site (cite it). **Cede to the owning rule**: `dispose-leak` owns releasing owned disposables on every path; this rule owns pruning cache entries whose captured component or element ref was cleared to null, independently of the domain-item prune. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW or changed component / element ref caches; for each prune path, verify it covers BOTH the domain-item-gone case AND the captured-ref-cleared-to-null case; flag when only the domain-item case is pruned and a null-ref entry can persist. | | LOW |
| design-token-colors-not-hardcoded | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified component style in the diff (a `.razor.css` / `.css` / `.scss` rule, an inline `style=` attribute, or a class that sets a literal color) that assigns a COLOR property (`color`, `background` / `background-color`, `border-color`, `fill`, `stroke`, or a shadow color) to a HARD-CODED literal (a hex code, `rgb(...)` / `rgba(...)`, or a named color such as `red`) WHERE the codebase already exposes a THEME or DESIGN-TOKEN system (CSS custom properties like `var(--token)`, a theme variable, or a SCSS `$token`): verify the color derives from the token or theme so it tracks theme changes (light / dark / high-contrast) instead of drifting. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a new component sets `color: #333;` and `background: #fff;` as literals while sibling components use `var(--app-text)` and `var(--app-surface)` - the hard-coded colors do not follow a dark-theme switch and drift out of sync. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: a color with no token equivalent that is documented as intentionally fixed (cite it); a one-off non-theme surface (a brand logo color, a fixed scrim / overlay); the codebase has no token / theme system to derive from (cite). **Cede to the owning rule**: `sibling-variant-consistency` owns a general odd-one-out across two or more sibling members; this rule owns deriving a color from the established theme or design-token system instead of a hard-coded literal. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW color declarations in changed styles; for each literal color, check whether the codebase exposes a theme token for that role; flag a hard-coded color where a token exists, and flag-for-manual when the token inventory is out of the diff (require the author to confirm no token equivalent exists). | | LOW |
| component-icon-labels-need-explicit-spacing | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified REUSABLE component in the diff that renders an ICON adjacent to TEXT (an icon component plus a label, an SVG or glyph plus a caption, a button or menu item with both an icon and text): verify there is EXPLICIT spacing between the icon and the text (a margin / padding, a flex or grid `gap`, a non-breaking space, or a dedicated spacer element), so the label does not visually run into the icon - especially because whitespace between Razor elements can be collapsed or absent in the rendered output. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a reusable button rendering an icon immediately followed by the label with no gap, so the text abuts the icon glyph; a menu item with an icon and text that lacks any `gap` or margin between them. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the icon component already includes its own trailing spacing (cite it); a deliberate tight / no-gap design (cite the intent); a single-use (non-reusable) layout where spacing is handled inline at the call site. **Cede to the owning rule**: `sibling-variant-consistency` owns an odd-one-out where sibling components space their icons but this one does not; this rule owns inserting explicit icon-to-label spacing in a reusable component. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW reusable components that render an icon next to text; for each, verify an explicit spacing mechanism separates the icon and the label; flag when the icon and text are adjacent with no gap, margin, spacer, or non-breaking space. | | LOW |
| markdown-placeholders-must-not-look-like-html-tags | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified Markdown document, README, or markdown-rendered comment in the diff that contains an ANGLE-BRACKET PLACEHOLDER (a token like `<name>`, `<your-token>`, `<path>`, or a generic like `<T>`) in PROSE OUTSIDE a code span or fenced block: verify the placeholder is ESCAPED (`&lt;name&gt;`) or CODE-FORMATTED (wrapped in backticks or a fenced block) so a Markdown renderer does not silently swallow it as an unknown HTML tag and drop it from the rendered output. **High-signal failure**: PR-era a README with `Run foo --out <output-dir>` written in prose - a GitHub-flavored-markdown renderer treats `<output-dir>` as an unknown HTML tag and it vanishes from the rendered page; a doc using bare `<T>` generic placeholders that disappear when rendered. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the angle-bracket text IS intended HTML that the renderer should process (cite the real tag); the placeholder is already inside a code span or fenced block; a renderer configured to escape raw HTML (cite the config). **Cede to the owning rule**: `doc-impl-mismatch` owns a prose claim falsified by the code; this rule owns an angle-bracket placeholder in Markdown prose that a renderer swallows as an HTML tag (a rendering / formatting defect, not a claim falsification). **Audit method**: enumerate NEW Markdown prose outside code spans and fenced blocks; for each `<...>` angle-bracket token that is a placeholder rather than a real HTML tag, flag when it is unescaped and not code-formatted, and flag-for-manual when whether the token is intended HTML is ambiguous (require the author to confirm it is a placeholder). | | LOW |
| git-diff-path-parsing-requires-quote-path-false | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified invocation in the diff that runs `git diff` / `git diff-tree` and PARSES the output for FILE PATHS (`--name-only`, `--name-status`) or parses the `+++ b/<path>` content headers (`-U0` / `--unified=0`): verify it passes `-c core.quotePath=false` (a git global option, before the subcommand). Without it git OCTAL-ESCAPES and double-quotes any path containing non-ASCII bytes (`"b/\303\274ber.cs"`), so a path / header parser silently MISSES that file - a fail-open in tooling that gates, classifies, or audits by path. **High-signal failure**: a pre-commit or CI script doing `git diff --cached --name-only` with no `-c core.quotePath=false` to decide which files a gate covers - an added or renamed file with an accented name is octal-escaped and skips the gate. Caveat for `+++`-header parsers: even WITH the flag, git appends a disambiguation TAB to a space-containing path and fully quotes a path with an embedded quote, backslash, or control char, so the parser must also stop the capture at the tab (`[^\t]+`) and tolerate the always-quoted form. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the diff output is consumed for CONTENT only and never keyed by path (the target file is already known); the invocation routes through a wrapper that injects the flag (cite it); the paths are guaranteed ASCII by a documented invariant (cite it). **Cede to the owning rule**: `loop-silently-drops-failed-items` owns unsignalled skips from already-parsed input; this rule owns the upstream git-quoting that makes a path-parse silently incomplete. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW `git diff` / `diff-tree` invocations whose output is parsed for paths or `+++` headers; for each, verify `-c core.quotePath=false` is present or injected by the called wrapper; flag when absent, and flag-for-manual when the path-versus-content usage is ambiguous (require the author to confirm). | | MEDIUM |
| interactive-control-lacks-an-accessible-name | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified interactive control in the diff, per the `html.instructions.md` rule on `aria-label` and the `coding-standards.instructions.md` rule that accessibility text describes behavior not appearance, verify the control has an accessible name when visible text or an associated label does not already provide one, and that the name describes what the control DOES. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: icon-only `<button>` with no text and no `aria-label`; custom select, menu, disclosure, or clickable element with no label; `aria-label="X icon"` or `"Filter icon"` that describes appearance; duplicated wording such as `"Filter filter sets by tag"`; stale label text after the button action changed. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the control already has clear visible text; an associated `<label for=...>` or `aria-labelledby` supplies the name; a redundant `aria-label` was intentionally avoided because it would override visible text or be superseded by `aria-labelledby`; non-interactive decorative icons are hidden from assistive tech. **Cede to the owning rule**: `aria-relationships-reference-mounted-targets` owns ARIA id-reference relationships pointing at mounted targets; `aria-owned-role-structure-must-be-valid` owns container-role / required-child-role structural validity; `keyboard-item-actions-must-be-reachable` owns keyboard reachability of item actions; this rule owns the accessible NAME being missing, wrong, duplicated, stale, or appearance-only. **Audit method**: enumerate added or modified interactive elements (`button`, `a[href]`, form controls, custom elements with click handlers, `role="button"`, `role="combobox"`, `tabindex`, or component parameters such as `AriaLabel`); for each unlabeled or icon-only control, extract the accessible-name source in priority order: visible text, associated `<label>`, `aria-labelledby`, then `aria-label`; read the final name aloud and flag when it is missing, duplicated, stale, or appearance-only. | | MEDIUM |
| native-error-omitted-from-thrown-message | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified Win32, HRESULT, P/Invoke, or other native-error wrapper in the diff, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` rule on forwarding resolved native messages and the `coding-standards-code.instructions.md` rule that exception messages stay diagnostic, verify the thrown managed exception includes the resolved native message or richer context containing the API name, native code, and relevant input. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: one switch branch throws `new UnauthorizedAccessException()` while sibling branches pass the resolved message; the code maps a native error to `FileNotFoundException`, `InvalidDataException`, `OperationCanceledException`, or raw `Exception` with no message; a composed message drops the API name, path, handle, or error code that the catch site needs. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the exception is not wrapping a native or HRESULT failure; the branch delegates to a helper that already captures and includes the native details; the exception type or helper API is passed the native code and reliably formats the diagnostic message; a parameterless exception is used only for an internal invariant unrelated to native failure. **Cede to the owning rule**: `message-asserts-outcome-not-guaranteed-on-every-branch` owns general runtime user-facing, log, or exception strings whose claimed outcome is not true on every emitting branch; `failure-outcome-diagnostics-preserve-specific-cause` owns structured failure outcomes that collapse the specific cause, terminal state, or originating token; this rule owns NATIVE / Win32 / HRESULT resolved-message forwarding specifically. **Audit method**: enumerate changed native-error mapping helpers, P/Invoke failure branches, and HRESULT switches; for every `throw new ...Exception(...)` branch, check whether the resolved native message (or a composed message carrying the API name and error code) is passed; flag any branch that throws a parameterless or message-less exception or drops the native diagnostic detail. | | LOW |
| persisted-enum-changed-non-additively | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified enum in the diff that may be persisted, serialized, exported, stored in settings, written to disk, or exchanged across versions by numeric value, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` rule on non-additive persisted enum changes, verify existing members keep their numeric meanings. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: inserting a member anywhere except the end of an implicit-value enum; reordering members; removing a member; renumbering an existing member; clearing saved state instead of preserving or migrating old values; assuming tests that only cover fresh data prove persisted-data compatibility. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the enum is process-local and never persisted or serialized by numeric value, with that lifetime cited; the change is append-only at the end and existing numeric values are unchanged; all existing members have explicit numeric values that remain unchanged; a same-PR migration or compatibility reader maps old values correctly. **Cede to the owning rule**: `serialized-discriminator-rename-accepts-legacy-values` owns string / textual serialization discriminator renames and legacy-value acceptance; this rule owns the NUMERIC enum value being renumbered, reordered, or removed for persisted / serialized data. **Audit method**: enumerate enum diffs; for each changed enum, grep its name and member names through serializers, settings, config, database, registry, export/import, JSON converters, binary readers, and tests; determine whether numeric values can already exist outside the current process; flag non-additive edits unless unchanged explicit values, append-only semantics, or a migration covers every persisted value. | | LOW |
| dictionary-enumeration-used-for-display-or-serialized-order | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified `Dictionary` or `ImmutableDictionary` enumeration in the diff whose resulting sequence drives UI, markdown, clipboard, exported files, JSON arrays, snapshots, or other observable order, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` rule on dictionaries driving display or serialization order, verify the code applies a deterministic order before rendering or serializing. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: projecting `dictionary.Values` directly into rows; using `ImmutableDictionary` as the source for columns or menu items; relying on current `Dictionary` insertion behavior; fallback code that enumerates a lookup dictionary when a configured order is missing; serializing dictionary entries into an array without `OrderBy` or a domain order. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the dictionary is used only for lookup or set membership; the output is itself a key-value map whose order is not part of the contract; an explicit `OrderBy`, domain order, or ordered source list controls the emitted sequence; tests intentionally assert membership without relying on sequence order. **Cede to the owning rule**: `groupby-output-rendered-without-explicit-ordering` owns `GroupBy` / LINQ-projection results consumed as stably ordered; this rule owns a `Dictionary` / `ImmutableDictionary` enumeration driving observable order. **Audit method**: enumerate added or modified `(Immutable)Dictionary` creations and enumerations (`Keys`, `Values`, `foreach`, `.Select`, `.ToList`, serialization loops); follow each sequence to its consumer; flag when the consumer observes order and no explicit sort, default-order list, enum-order mapping, or other documented deterministic ordering is applied before output. | | LOW |
| public-test-seeding-helper-on-production-type | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW public `With*`, `Seed*`, `Build*`, `For*Test*`, or similar mutation/seeding helper added to a production type, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` rule on public test-seeding helpers on production types, verify it is a genuine production API rather than test-only surface. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: a public helper added to a production `record` or `sealed class` only so tests can set otherwise protected state; a helper that bypasses invariants such as single-owner, required metadata, validation, or lifecycle rules; a method referenced only from test projects; a builder-like method whose name or body exposes test-fixture intent. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the method is part of a documented public builder or immutable `with`-style API used by production callers; the helper is `internal` with a deliberate `InternalsVisibleTo` test seam; the seeding helper lives in a test/shared-test assembly; the member preserves all production invariants and has non-test production use. **Cede to the owning rule**: `friend-grant-or-keep-public-when-di-seam-available` owns the general visibility / DI-seam choice for cross-assembly construction and InternalsVisibleTo decisions; this rule owns a public test-only seeding or mutation helper leaking onto a production type. **Audit method**: enumerate new public members on production types, especially names beginning with `With`, `Seed`, `Build`, `Create`, or containing `Test`; grep call sites across production and tests; inspect whether the helper mutates or seeds state that normal constructors or factories intentionally protect; flag when all real usage is test-only or when production callers could bypass invariants. | | LOW |
| groupby-output-rendered-without-explicit-ordering | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified LINQ `GroupBy` pipeline whose result sequence drives rendered, serialized, clipboard, markdown, table, or other observable output, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` rule on `GroupBy` consumed as stable without explicit `OrderBy`, verify the groups are explicitly ordered by the domain order before output. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: `.GroupBy(...).Select(...)` rendered as rows with no ordering; assuming enum declaration order without sorting by enum value or a declared sort key; grouping an input sequence whose order varies by filesystem, dictionary, database, or async arrival; tests passing because fixture input happens to be sorted. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: group order genuinely does not matter because the result is a lookup, set, or order-insensitive assertion; an explicit order is applied before output; the source is already sorted by the same domain key and that upstream ordering is cited; only item order within each group matters and is separately ordered or order-insensitive. **Cede to the owning rule**: `dictionary-enumeration-used-for-display-or-serialized-order` owns `Dictionary` / `ImmutableDictionary` enumeration driving observable order; this rule owns a `GroupBy` / LINQ-projection result consumed as stably ordered. **Audit method**: enumerate added or modified `.GroupBy(` calls; for each, follow the grouped sequence to its materialization and consumer; flag when output order is observable and no `.OrderBy`, `.OrderByDescending`, domain-order comparer, or cited upstream sort appears between grouping and output. | | LOW |
| wrapper-validation-reports-the-downstream-parameter-name | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified factory, wrapper, adapter, or convenience method that validates caller input by delegating to a downstream constructor or helper, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` rule on `ArgumentException` `paramName` identifying the wrong parameter, verify any thrown `ArgumentException`, `ArgumentNullException`, `ArgumentOutOfRangeException`, or test assertion reports the caller-facing parameter name. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: wrapper parameter `prefix` is transformed into downstream parameter `value`, and the downstream constructor throws `paramName: "value"`; a `Create*` or `New*` method performs no upfront guard and lets an inner type expose its internal parameter name; tests assert the downstream name, baking in the wrong public contract; exception text tells callers to fix a parameter they never supplied. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the wrapper forwards the same parameter without transformation and the downstream name is genuinely caller-facing; the wrapper adds an upfront guard with `nameof(callerParameter)` before delegation; the API intentionally exposes the downstream parameter contract and documents it; the thrown exception is not an argument-validation failure. **Cede to the owning rule**: `message-asserts-outcome-not-guaranteed-on-every-branch` owns the general truthfulness of a runtime exception or log message's text on every branch; this rule owns specifically the `ParamName` on a validating wrapper / factory / adapter naming the caller-facing parameter rather than the downstream / internal one. **Audit method**: enumerate changed wrapper, factory, `Create*`, `New*`, adapter, and overload-forwarding methods; map each public or caller-facing parameter to the downstream arguments it builds; inspect guards and exception assertions; flag when invalid caller input can surface an exception whose `ParamName` names only the downstream/internal parameter rather than the wrapper parameter. | | LOW |
| status-enum-distinguishes-every-branchable-outcome | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified status, result, outcome enum, discriminated union, or equivalent result code in the diff, per the `csharp-smells.instructions.md` universal-smell that status enums must distinguish every outcome a caller could branch on, verify semantically different caller actions have distinct values. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: EOF, empty-success, and read-failure all collapse to `Empty` or `None`; cancellation, timeout, and error all return `Failed`; partial success and complete success share `Success`; a boolean or two-value enum replaces a richer result even though callers log, retry, prompt, or fallback differently; downstream switches infer missing detail from side channels such as null payloads or empty collections. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the collapsed outcomes are intentionally indistinguishable by public contract and no caller can act differently; a richer error/result object carries a separate discriminator for the branchable detail; the method throws for exceptional outcomes and the enum only represents successful states; the detail is unavailable at that abstraction boundary and the contract documents the loss. **Cede to the owning rule**: `failure-outcome-diagnostics-preserve-specific-cause` owns preserving the specific failure cause, terminal state, and originating token in a reported outcome; this rule owns a status / result enum collapsing outcomes a caller would branch on into one value. **Audit method**: enumerate changed result/status types and methods returning them; list every observable outcome the method can produce from the diff and direct callees; inspect callers, logs, retries, UI branches, and tests for different handling needs; flag when two outcomes that a reasonable caller could branch on are represented by the same enum value or by payload shape instead of the discriminator. | | LOW |
| intentional-narrowing-cast-not-explicit-unchecked | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified explicit integer cast in the diff that deliberately narrows or bit-reinterprets a value (`long` to `int`, `int` to `short`, `uint` to `int`, or similar), verify wraparound or sign reinterpretation is written as `unchecked((Target)value)` rather than a plain cast, unless the value is provably in range or overflow checking is intentionally required. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: a hash, checksum, binary-format, interop, or bit-packing path uses `(int)value` where high bits are intentionally truncated; sibling code already uses `unchecked(...)` for the same narrowing idiom but the new site does not; a helper relies on the default unchecked project context and would start throwing `OverflowException` if `CheckForOverflowUnderflow` is enabled or the expression moves under a `checked` block. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the source value is provably constrained to the target type's range before the cast; the cast is inside an explicit `unchecked` block that covers the expression; a checked overflow exception is the intended contract and is documented or enforced by surrounding code; the cast is a widening conversion or a non-numeric cast. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW or modified explicit numeric casts; for each narrowing or signedness-changing integer cast, trace the value range and intent from nearby code and sibling idioms; flag when wraparound or reinterpretation is intended but the expression is not explicitly `unchecked`, and flag-for-manual when intent or range is ambiguous. | | MEDIUM |
| fail-closed-parser-case-sensitive-token-match | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified parser or dispatcher in the diff whose contract says non-canonical input fails closed, verify recognized tokens are matched with the intended case sensitivity and that token detection and the corresponding assignment or dispatch use the SAME comparison. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: a parser recognizes markup, manifest, enum-like, keyword, or field-name tokens with `==`, `Equals` without a `StringComparison`, or an ordinal case-sensitive comparer even though producers or specs treat token casing as flexible; a pre-check uses case-insensitive matching but the assignment switch uses case-sensitive matching, or the reverse; a mis-cased-but-valid token silently falls through to a raw fallback, skips a field, or collapses distinct inputs into the same result. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the token language is genuinely case-sensitive and the rule or spec is cited; the parser normalizes tokens before both detection and dispatch; both detection and assignment deliberately share the same comparer; the token is an opaque user value rather than a recognized grammar token. **Cede to the owning rule**: `fail-closed-parser-returns-canonical-node-on-empty-parse` owns returning a canonical/default node when no non-empty identifying field was captured; this rule owns case-sensitive token matching and comparer mismatches in fail-closed parsers. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW or modified token comparisons in fail-closed parsers or dispatchers; for each recognized token, trace both the detection path and the assignment/dispatch path; flag when casing can change recognition contrary to the contract, or when the two paths use different comparison semantics. | | MEDIUM |
| structured-text-injection-escapes-only-some-values | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified construction of structured text in the diff that injects two or more values into XML, HTML, JSON, SQL, shell-like syntax, or another grammar-bearing format, verify EVERY injected value is escaped or encoded for that target grammar and that any write-side escape which later participates in raw-keyed lookup has a matching read-side decode. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: one injected value is passed through an XML/HTML/JSON/SQL encoder while a sibling attribute, element text, name, key, filter, or clause value is concatenated raw; escaping the visually obvious field makes the surrounding raw field look reviewed; a persisted escaped value is later used to look up data keyed by the original raw value, causing silent misses; a custom escape is applied only on write with no round-trip decode. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: every injected value uses the correct encoder for the target grammar; a value is proven to come from a constrained alphabet that excludes grammar-special characters; a structured writer/API is used instead of string concatenation; the escaped stored form is intentionally the lookup key and all readers use that same escaped representation. **Cede to the owning rule** (do not duplicate-flag): `delimited-composite-keys-must-escape-values` owns composite-key collision from delimiter-joined values; `xss-injection-risk` owns general single-value HTML, JavaScript, SQL, and shell injection; this rule owns the multi-value asymmetric/partial-escaping case (2+ injected values where some are escaped and a sibling is injected raw), XML and JSON structured-text validity, and the write-escape/read-decode round-trip mismatch. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW or modified structured-text builders and serializers that inject multiple values; list each injected value and its encoder or proof of safety; flag any unescaped sibling value, wrong-grammar encoder, asymmetric custom escape, or write-side escape that round-trips into a raw-keyed lookup without a decode. | | MEDIUM |
| fail-closed-parser-returns-canonical-node-on-empty-parse | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified parser or deserializer in the diff whose contract says non-canonical input fails closed to a raw or opaque representation, verify it returns the raw/opaque representation, not a default-valued canonical node, whenever it captured no non-empty field that identifies the parsed node. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: the parser initializes a canonical object and returns it even when no recognized field was present; recognized fields are present but all values are empty strings, whitespace-normalized empty values, null-equivalent values, or otherwise non-identifying; the canonical node serializes fields positionally, so distinct degenerate inputs produce identical all-empty canonical output, hashes, dedupe keys, or merge keys. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the canonical node legitimately has no required identifying fields and distinct raw inputs are not expected to remain distinct; the parser stores enough raw source data inside the canonical representation to preserve identity; empty fields are valid identifiers under a documented format contract; the path is a strict canonical producer that cannot receive non-canonical or degenerate input. **Cede to the owning rule**: `fail-closed-parser-case-sensitive-token-match` owns whether recognized tokens are matched with the correct case sensitivity and consistent comparers; this rule owns the all-empty parse that incorrectly returns a canonical/default node. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW or modified fail-closed parsers/deserializers; identify the fields that give the canonical node identity; trace both cases where no recognized field is present and where recognized fields are present but all identifying values are empty; flag when either case returns the canonical node instead of the raw/opaque representation. | | MEDIUM |
| equality-serializes-operands-to-throwaway-buffers-on-hot-path | review-pass-only | {} | For every NEW or modified equality, comparison, comparer, or comparator in the diff that runs on a per-item hot path, verify it does not compare by fully serializing both operands into throwaway buffers on every call when a streaming lockstep compare with an equal-prefix fast path and early exit would preserve behavior. **Common failure modes / High-signal patterns**: `Equals`, `Compare`, `IEqualityComparer<T>`, sort comparison, merge, dedupe, or lookup code allocates two `ArrayBufferWriter`, `MemoryStream`, `StringBuilder`, strings, byte arrays, or similar buffers only to compare their serialized contents; the method is called once per row, node, candidate pair, sort comparison, or merge step; operands that are equal by reference or differ in an early field still pay full serialization cost; an optimization compares raw in-memory text even though the hash/key uses encoded bytes, risking drift. **Acceptable (do NOT flag)**: the path is cold, diagnostic, setup-only, or bounded to tiny fixed operands; serialization does not allocate or is reused safely across calls; the serialized form is already required by the caller and the comparison reuses it; a byte-exact streaming compare would be more complex than justified for a documented small input; the existing serializer is the only reliable definition of equality and no hot-path cost is present. **Cede to the owning rule**: `perf-batching` owns general repeated scans, batching, materialization, and hot-path allocation; this rule owns equality/comparison code that serializes both operands into throwaway buffers per call. **Audit method**: enumerate NEW or modified equality/comparison methods and comparer delegates; determine call frequency and operand size from the diff and direct callers; flag hot-path full serialization of both operands when a same-semantics streaming lockstep compare can early-exit, and if serialization also defines a hash or key, require the proposed compare to be byte-exact with that serializer, including lossy encoding or replacement behavior. | | MEDIUM |

## Patterns (deterministic checker-scoped - mechanized by a registry checker; logic lives in the script, not rg/lens)

These slugs are enforced by a script named in `data/checker-registry.tsv` (parity-gated by `scripts/check-checker-registry.ps1`), NOT by an rg pattern or a lens prompt - their detection logic is too contextual for a single regex but too mechanical to spend a reviewer slot on. `params.checker_id` names the checker. The gate-runner does not rg these (it emits a `checker-mechanized` marker); `invoke-panel.ps1` does not forward them to the lens. They live in the catalog so it stays the single inventory of every enforced rule.

| slug | scope_mode | params | review_pass_only_prompt | fp_slug | tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| receipt-numeric-claim-drift | checker-scoped | {"checker_id":"check-diff-consistency"} |  |  | HIGH |
| shell-sed-exit-zero-fallback-unreachable | checker-scoped | {"checker_id":"check-diff-consistency"} |  |  | LOW |
| ci-shell-or-true-swallows-real-failures | checker-scoped | {"checker_id":"check-diff-consistency"} |  |  | LOW |
| ci-jq-arg-literal-backslash-n | checker-scoped | {"checker_id":"check-diff-consistency"} |  |  | LOW |

---

## FP-1: parent-namespace-not-in-scope

**Technical claim**: a child namespace requires an explicit `using` directive to reference an identifier declared in its parent namespace.

**Why FP**: Per C# spec ECMA-334 § 13.8 (Namespace declarations) / C# 7 spec § 3.8 (Namespace and type names), simple-name lookup walks enclosing namespaces from innermost to outermost. A type declared in a parent namespace is visible from any descendant namespace without a `using` directive. Build green = lookup succeeded = claim is wrong.

**Recurrence pattern**: tends to recur across multiple PRs touching the same parent/child namespace pair; Copilot's pattern matcher doesn't apply the C# language rule consistently. Phrasing varies ("isn't in scope", "won't compile", "requires explicit using") - match on technical claim, not exact wording.

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive - C# child namespaces have implicit access to parent-namespace identifiers per ECMA-334 § 13.8 / C# 7 spec § 3.8. The file's namespace is a descendant of the target type's namespace; no `using` directive is required. Build green at the cited commit confirms the lookup succeeds. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-1.

**Mitigation candidates** (deferred for low FP cost): add explicit `using <parent-namespace>;` per file (1 line per file; reduces FP but pollutes the using list); move type into descendant namespace if domain semantics permit (forces a namespace where it doesn't semantically belong). Status: project-by-project decision.

## FP-2: await-using-on-idisposable-only-type

**Technical claim**: the type returned at the use site implements `IDisposable` but NOT `IAsyncDisposable`, so `await using` will not compile.

**Why FP**: agent must verify the actual type at the use site. Specifically:
- `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.ServiceProvider` - implements BOTH `IDisposable` AND `IAsyncDisposable` since .NET Core 3.0 (2019). See [ServiceProvider class reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.dependencyinjection.serviceprovider).
- `System.IO.Stream` - implements BOTH since .NET Core 3.0. See [Stream.DisposeAsync](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.io.stream.disposeasync).
- `System.Threading.SemaphoreSlim` and `System.Net.Http.HttpClient` are `IDisposable`-only as of .NET 8; do NOT add them to the dismissal list.

Build evidence: if `await using var x = ...` compiles, the type implements `IAsyncDisposable`. Period.

**Recurrence pattern**: Copilot's pattern matcher appears to have stale framework knowledge about which BCL types implement `IAsyncDisposable`. Tends to recur in clusters (all N CLI command files in a single review batch).

**Canonical dismissal template**:
> False positive - `<TypeName>` implements both `IDisposable` AND `IAsyncDisposable` since `<framework-version>` (cite: `<docs-url>`). Project targets `<consuming-project-tfm>`. `await using` compiles correctly. Build green at the cited commit confirms. Recurring FP per `pattern-catalog.md` FP-2.

**Mitigation candidates**: none worth pursuing. Fix is on Copilot's side. Optional: explicit type annotation at the use site (`await using ServiceProvider sp = ...`) makes the interface implementation more obvious to Copilot's matcher - may reduce FP recurrence.

---

## Catalog maintenance

After each PR final state (converged-to-zero OR merged OR closed-with-deferred-findings-tracker):

1. Append findings to the global `data/findings.csv` (per its schema).
2. Re-run the regex classifier against the corpus. **Catalog-first:** add EVERY generalizable finding to this catalog immediately (1-hit) with abstract phrasing (PR to CopilotInstructions) - do NOT wait for a frequency threshold. Frequency drives TIER, not inclusion (see Tier semantics).
3. If a pattern's hit rate is decaying (fewer hits round-over-round), demote it from high-frequency battery to lower-frequency section. Demotions require ≥2 consecutive rounds of zero-hits to confirm decay.
4. Update FP entries if Copilot raises a new FP worth deterring (≥2 recurrences + source-grounded dismissal).
5. Commit catalog updates to `CopilotInstructions/<branch>`. Catalog is project-deidentified; no per-project data ever lands here.
